Haiden (March 2017) | map_type = Japan Miyazaki | latd = 31 | latm = 56 | lats = 13.67 | latNS = N | longd = 131 | longm = 25 | longs = 18.35 | longEW = E | coordinates_display = inline,title | coordinates_region = JP | location = 2-4-3 Jingu, Miyazaki City, Miyazaki Prefecture | deity = Spirits of those who served the nation and public from Miyazaki Prefecture, totaling 41,874 (as of March 2016)"70th Anniversary of the End of the Greater East Asia War" Memorial Book, p. 8 | founded = March 10, 1955 | annual_festival = April 10 | website = [https://miyazaki-gokoku.jp/ Miyazaki Gokoku Shrine] }} [[File:Miyazakiken-Gokoku-jinja, Mitama festival 01.jpg|thumb|250px|Lanterns for the Mitama festival in the shrine grounds]] {{nihongo|'''Miyazaki Gokoku Shrine'''|宮崎縣護國神社|Miyazaki-ken Gokoku Jinja}} is a {{ill|Gokoku shrine|en|護国神社|12=draft|12=draft}} located in {{ill|Miyazaki, Miyazaki|en|宮崎市|12=draft|12=draft}}. The shrine enshrines the spirits of 41,820 individuals who served the nation and public from Miyazaki Prefecture since the {{nihongo|Boshin War|戊辰戦争|Boshin sensō}}, along with 54 public servants who died in the line of duty (as of March 2016). == Overview == Most of the Gokoku shrines across Japan were originally established as {{ill|Shokonsha|en|招魂社|12=draft|12=draft}} during the {{nihongo|Meiji Restoration|明治維新|Meiji Ishin}}, with rituals for memorial services conducted before World War II. However, due to the fragmented state of Miyazaki Prefecture during the Meiji RestorationAt the time, the Miyazaki area ({{nihongo|Hyuga Province|日向国|Hyūga no kuni}}) was divided into various territories such as the {{nihongo|Bakufu|天領|}}, {{nihongo|Nobeoka Domain|延岡藩|}}, {{nihongo|Takanabe Domain|高鍋藩|}}, {{nihongo|Sadowara Domain|佐土原藩|}}, {{nihongo|Obi Domain|飫肥藩|}}, and {{nihongo|Satsuma Domain|薩摩藩|}}., there was no single Shokonsha to enshrine the spirits of those who died for the nation across the entire prefecture. Instead, small portable shrines were used for annual memorial services. Before World War II, there were seven Shokonsha shrines in Miyazaki Prefecture"Sociologica 27", pp. 36-37. They were all renamed Gokoku shrines on April 1, 1939, following an Interior Ministry order"Renaming Shokonsha to Gokoku Shrines" (Showa 14 Interior Ministry Ordinance No. 12) "Official Gazette" No. 3656, March 15, 1939, p. 510 ({{NDLJP|2960149/2}}). However, none of them were designated by the Interior Minister as official Gokoku shrines and were instead considered unofficial Gokoku shrines"Number of Gokoku Shrines" "Outline of Interior Ministry Affairs Showa 16", Interior Ministry, February 20, 1942, pp. 375-376 ({{NDLJP|1450040/571}}). {| class="wikitable" ! Shrine name (as of March 1942) ! location (then) ! location (now) ! Notes |- | Hirose Gokoku Shrine | Kitanaka District, Hirose Village | Sadowara Town, Miyazaki City | Originally Naka Shimoshima Shokonsha, founded by Sadowara Domain Lord {{nihongo|Shimazu Tadaoki|島津忠寛|}} |- | Fukushima Gokoku Shrine | Minaminaka District, Fukushima Town | Nishikata, Kushima City | Originally Nishikata Shokonsha, located on the rear mound of Fukushima Kofun No. 4 (Kenjōzuka) |- | Miyakonojo Gokoku Shrine | Miyamaru Town, Miyakonojo City | Komatsubara Town, Miyakonojo City (abolished) | Originally Miyamaru Shokonsha, dissolved as a religious corporation on October 2, 1956"Eighty Years of Miyazaki Prefecture Governance, Vol. 1", Miyazaki Prefecture, March 31, 1967, p. 883 ({{NDLJP|3030426/510}}) |- | Takaoka Gokoku Shrine | Higashimorokata District, Takaoka Town | Takaoka Town, Miyazaki City | Originally Morokata Uchiyama Shokonsha |- | Oyamada Gokoku Shrine (Muka Gokoku Shrine) | Higashimorokata District, Muka Village | Takaoka Town, Miyazaki City | Originally Morokata Muka Shokonsha, founded at Muka Castle site |- | Takanabe Gokoku Shrine | Koyu District, Takanabe Town | Same | Originally Takanabe Shokonsha, founded at Takanabe Castle site by Takanabe Domain |- | Aya Gokoku Shrine | Higashimorokata District, Aya VillageAya Town was established as a town on October 1, 1932, but the source follows the original designation. | Aya Town, Higashimorokata District | Privately established Shokonsha |} With the outbreak of the {{nihongo|Greater East Asia War|大東亜戦争|Daitōa sensō}} on December 8, 1941, the number of war dead increased significantly, deepening the public’s respect and gratitude for the spirits of the fallen. Consequently, the governor of Miyazaki Prefecture, {{nihongo|Nagafune Katsumi|長船克己|}}, and 58 representatives from various sectors formed the Miyazaki Prefecture Gokoku Shrine Construction Association. Although there were calls to establish the shrine in different parts of the prefecture, it was ultimately decided to build it in the prefectural capital, Miyazaki City, which lacked a designated Gokoku Shrine. On April 23, 1943, Interior Minister {{nihongo|Ando Kisaburo|安藤紀三郎|}} approved the establishment of the Miyazaki Gokoku Shrine to enshrine 3,358 spirits, with the groundbreaking ceremony held on May 10, 1943. Construction continued despite laborious efforts and material shortages, but the project was halted on May 31, 1945, due to intensified air raids. Following Japan’s defeat, the shrine construction was forcibly abandoned under the orders of the GHQ’s Miyazaki Civil Affairs Chief, Navy Lieutenant Masman. After the Treaty of San Francisco in 1952, efforts to rebuild the shrine resumed. Governor {{nihongo|Tanaka Nagashige|田中長茂|}} led the reorganization of the Miyazaki Prefecture Gokoku Shrine Rebuilding Association, which secured public donations to fund the project. On June 13, 1954, it was decided to establish the shrine within the precincts of {{nihongo|Miyazaki Jingu|宮崎神宮|}}, and the shrine was officially created on March 10, 1955. Subsequently, it was registered as a religious corporation on December 24, 1955[https://www.m-shinsei.jp/m_shrine/宮崎県護國神社（みやざきけんごこくじんじゃ）/ Miyazaki Gokoku Shrine (宮崎県護國神社)] Miyazaki Shrine Introduction Site, Miyazaki Prefecture Shinto Youth Association, April 20, 2012. == Facilities == [[File:View in Miyazaki Prefecture Gokoku Shrine.jpg|thumb|250px|Memorial monument within Miyazaki Gokoku Shrine (March 2017)]] * Relics Hall * Memorial monument for the war dead from the {{ill|Bougainville Island|en|ブーゲンビル島|12=draft|12=draft}} campaign * Monument for war dead from the 1st Taiwan Infantry Regiment from Miyazaki Prefecture * Monument for the war dead from the {{ill|37th Division (Imperial Japanese Army)|en|第37師団 (日本軍)|12=draft|12=draft}} * Monument for the 3710th Unit * Memorial for the Five Kamikaze Heroes of Shikishima Unit, including {{nihongo|Yukio Seki|関行男|}}, {{nihongo|Iwao Nakano|中野磐雄|}}, {{nihongo|Nobuo Tani|谷暢夫|}}, {{nihongo|Hajime Nagamine|永峰肇|}}, and {{nihongo|Shigeo Oguro|大黒繁男|}} * Monument for the {{nihongo|72nd Infantry Regiment|歩兵第72連隊|}} * Statue of the Kamikaze Heroes File:Miyazakiken-Gokoku-jinja, Memorial Center 01.jpg|Relics Hall == Relation with Miyazaki Jingu == Initially built in the western forest of Miyazaki Jingu, the shrine was operated jointly with Miyazaki Jingu. However, due to the wishes of many citizens, including bereaved families, for independent management, dedicated staff were appointed in April 1972. == festivals == * Grand festival (April 10) ** Initially held on March 10 to commemorate the shrine’s founding and enshrinement, but moved to April 10 in 1978 to accommodate better attendance and the schedules of key officials[https://miyazakijingu.or.jp/publics/index/32/detail=1/b_id=128/r_id=340/ Memorial] Miyazaki Jingu Blog, April 11, 2018. * Mitama festival (August 14-15) * Daily Memorial Service (11:00 AM) == Nearby facilities == * {{ill|Miyazaki Jingū|ja|宮崎神宮|12=draft|12=draft}} == Access == * 10-minute walk from {{nihongo|Miyazaki Jingu Station|宮崎神宮駅|}} on the {{nihongo|Nippō Main Line|日豊本線|Nippō Honsen}} * 5-minute walk from the Miyazaki Jingu bus stop on the {{nihongo|Miyazaki Kōtsū|宮崎交通|}} bus route == References == {{reflist}} == Further Reading == * {{Cite book|和書|title=大東亜戦争終結七十年記念誌 |publisher=宮崎県神道青年会 |url=https://www.m-shinsei.jp/kanri/wp-content/uploads/2022/11/70nen.pdf |date=2016-08-15}} * {{Cite journal |和書|author=森幸雄 |title=護国神社を事例とする、都市のシンボル的施設に対するイメージの変遷 |url=https://hdl.handle.net/10911/2440 |journal=Sociologica |volume=27 |issue=第1,2合併号 |publisher=[[Soka University]] |date=2003rd year 3月 |pages=25-45 |naid=110006608846}} == Related topics == * {{nihongo|Kawanami Gokoku Shrine|川南護国神社|1=Kawaminami Gokoku Shrine|}} - A Gokoku shrine established in Kawanami Town, Koyu District, Miyazaki Prefecture after World War II == External links == {{Commonscat|Miyazakiken-Gokoku-jinja}} * [https://miyazaki-gokoku.jp/ Miyazaki Gokoku Shrine] - Haiden (March 2017) | map_type = Japan Miyazaki | latd = 31 | latm = 56 | lats = 13.67 | latNS = N | longd = 131 | longm = 25 | longs = 18.35 | longEW = E | coordinates_display = inline,title | coordinates_region = JP | location = 2-4-3 Jingu, Miyazaki City, Miyazaki Prefecture | deity = Spirits of those who served the nation and public from Miyazaki Prefecture, totaling 41,874 (as of March 2016)"70th Anniversary of the End of the Greater East Asia War" Memorial Book, p. 8 | founded = March 10, 1955 | annual_festival = April 10 | website = [https://miyazaki-gokoku.jp/ Miyazaki Gokoku Shrine] }} [[File:Miyazakiken-Gokoku-jinja, Mitama festival 01.jpg|thumb|250px|Lanterns for the Mitama festival in the shrine grounds]] {{nihongo|'''Miyazaki Gokoku Shrine'''|宮崎縣護國神社|Miyazaki-ken Gokoku Jinja}} is a {{ill|Gokoku shrine|en|護国神社|12=draft|12=draft}} located in {{ill|Miyazaki, Miyazaki|en|宮崎市|12=draft|12=draft}}. The shrine enshrines the spirits of 41,820 individuals who served the nation and public from Miyazaki Prefecture since the {{nihongo|Boshin War|戊辰戦争|Boshin sensō}}, along with 54 public servants who died in the line of duty (as of March 2016). == Overview == Most of the Gokoku shrines across Japan were originally established as {{ill|Shokonsha|en|招魂社|12=draft|12=draft}} during the {{nihongo|Meiji Restoration|明治維新|Meiji Ishin}}, with rituals for memorial services conducted before World War II. However, due to the fragmented state of Miyazaki Prefecture during the Meiji RestorationAt the time, the Miyazaki area ({{nihongo|Hyuga Province|日向国|Hyūga no kuni}}) was divided into various territories such as the {{nihongo|Bakufu|天領|}}, {{nihongo|Nobeoka Domain|延岡藩|}}, {{nihongo|Takanabe Domain|高鍋藩|}}, {{nihongo|Sadowara Domain|佐土原藩|}}, {{nihongo|Obi Domain|飫肥藩|}}, and {{nihongo|Satsuma Domain|薩摩藩|}}., there was no single Shokonsha to enshrine the spirits of those who died for the nation across the entire prefecture. Instead, small portable shrines were used for annual memorial services. Before World War II, there were seven Shokonsha shrines in Miyazaki Prefecture"Sociologica 27", pp. 36-37. They were all renamed Gokoku shrines on April 1, 1939, following an Interior Ministry order"Renaming Shokonsha to Gokoku Shrines" (Showa 14 Interior Ministry Ordinance No. 12) "Official Gazette" No. 3656, March 15, 1939, p. 510 ({{NDLJP|2960149/2}}). However, none of them were designated by the Interior Minister as official Gokoku shrines and were instead considered unofficial Gokoku shrines"Number of Gokoku Shrines" "Outline of Interior Ministry Affairs Showa 16", Interior Ministry, February 20, 1942, pp. 375-376 ({{NDLJP|1450040/571}}). {| class="wikitable" ! Shrine name (as of March 1942) ! location (then) ! location (now) ! Notes |- | Hirose Gokoku Shrine | Kitanaka District, Hirose Village | Sadowara Town, Miyazaki City | Originally Naka Shimoshima Shokonsha, founded by Sadowara Domain Lord {{nihongo|Shimazu Tadaoki|島津忠寛|}} |- | Fukushima Gokoku Shrine | Minaminaka District, Fukushima Town | Nishikata, Kushima City | Originally Nishikata Shokonsha, located on the rear mound of Fukushima Kofun No. 4 (Kenjōzuka) |- | Miyakonojo Gokoku Shrine | Miyamaru Town, Miyakonojo City | Komatsubara Town, Miyakonojo City (abolished) | Originally Miyamaru Shokonsha, dissolved as a religious corporation on October 2, 1956"Eighty Years of Miyazaki Prefecture Governance, Vol. 1", Miyazaki Prefecture, March 31, 1967, p. 883 ({{NDLJP|3030426/510}}) |- | Takaoka Gokoku Shrine | Higashimorokata District, Takaoka Town | Takaoka Town, Miyazaki City | Originally Morokata Uchiyama Shokonsha |- | Oyamada Gokoku Shrine (Muka Gokoku Shrine) | Higashimorokata District, Muka Village | Takaoka Town, Miyazaki City | Originally Morokata Muka Shokonsha, founded at Muka Castle site |- | Takanabe Gokoku Shrine | Koyu District, Takanabe Town | Same | Originally Takanabe Shokonsha, founded at Takanabe Castle site by Takanabe Domain |- | Aya Gokoku Shrine | Higashimorokata District, Aya VillageAya Town was established as a town on October 1, 1932, but the source follows the original designation. | Aya Town, Higashimorokata District | Privately established Shokonsha |} With the outbreak of the {{nihongo|Greater East Asia War|大東亜戦争|Daitōa sensō}} on December 8, 1941, the number of war dead increased significantly, deepening the public’s respect and gratitude for the spirits of the fallen. Consequently, the governor of Miyazaki Prefecture, {{nihongo|Nagafune Katsumi|長船克己|}}, and 58 representatives from various sectors formed the Miyazaki Prefecture Gokoku Shrine Construction Association. Although there were calls to establish the shrine in different parts of the prefecture, it was ultimately decided to build it in the prefectural capital, Miyazaki City, which lacked a designated Gokoku Shrine. On April 23, 1943, Interior Minister {{nihongo|Ando Kisaburo|安藤紀三郎|}} approved the establishment of the Miyazaki Gokoku Shrine to enshrine 3,358 spirits, with the groundbreaking ceremony held on May 10, 1943. Construction continued despite laborious efforts and material shortages, but the project was halted on May 31, 1945, due to intensified air raids. Following Japan’s defeat, the shrine construction was forcibly abandoned under the orders of the GHQ’s Miyazaki Civil Affairs Chief, Navy Lieutenant Masman. After the Treaty of San Francisco in 1952, efforts to rebuild the shrine resumed. Governor {{nihongo|Tanaka Nagashige|田中長茂|}} led the reorganization of the Miyazaki Prefecture Gokoku Shrine Rebuilding Association, which secured public donations to fund the project. On June 13, 1954, it was decided to establish the shrine within the precincts of {{nihongo|Miyazaki Jingu|宮崎神宮|}}, and the shrine was officially created on March 10, 1955. Subsequently, it was registered as a religious corporation on December 24, 1955[https://www.m-shinsei.jp/m_shrine/宮崎県護國神社（みやざきけんごこくじんじゃ）/ Miyazaki Gokoku Shrine (宮崎県護國神社)] Miyazaki Shrine Introduction Site, Miyazaki Prefecture Shinto Youth Association, April 20, 2012. == Facilities == [[File:View in Miyazaki Prefecture Gokoku Shrine.jpg|thumb|250px|Memorial monument within Miyazaki Gokoku Shrine (March 2017)]] * Relics Hall * Memorial monument for the war dead from the {{ill|Bougainville Island|en|ブーゲンビル島|12=draft|12=draft}} campaign * Monument for war dead from the 1st Taiwan Infantry Regiment from Miyazaki Prefecture * Monument for the war dead from the {{ill|37th Division (Imperial Japanese Army)|en|第37師団 (日本軍)|12=draft|12=draft}} * Monument for the 3710th Unit * Memorial for the Five Kamikaze Heroes of Shikishima Unit, including {{nihongo|Yukio Seki|関行男|}}, {{nihongo|Iwao Nakano|中野磐雄|}}, {{nihongo|Nobuo Tani|谷暢夫|}}, {{nihongo|Hajime Nagamine|永峰肇|}}, and {{nihongo|Shigeo Oguro|大黒繁男|}} * Monument for the {{nihongo|72nd Infantry Regiment|歩兵第72連隊|}} * Statue of the Kamikaze Heroes File:Miyazakiken-Gokoku-jinja, Memorial Center 01.jpg|Relics Hall == Relation with Miyazaki Jingu == Initially built in the western forest of Miyazaki Jingu, the shrine was operated jointly with Miyazaki Jingu. However, due to the wishes of many citizens, including bereaved families, for independent management, dedicated staff were appointed in April 1972. == festivals == * Grand festival (April 10) ** Initially held on March 10 to commemorate the shrine’s founding and enshrinement, but moved to April 10 in 1978 to accommodate better attendance and the schedules of key officials[https://miyazakijingu.or.jp/publics/index/32/detail=1/b_id=128/r_id=340/ Memorial] Miyazaki Jingu Blog, April 11, 2018. * Mitama festival (August 14-15) * Daily Memorial Service (11:00 AM) == Nearby facilities == * {{ill|Miyazaki Jingū|ja|宮崎神宮|12=draft|12=draft}} == Access == * 10-minute walk from {{nihongo|Miyazaki Jingu Station|宮崎神宮駅|}} on the {{nihongo|Nippō Main Line|日豊本線|Nippō Honsen}} * 5-minute walk from the Miyazaki Jingu bus stop on the {{nihongo|Miyazaki Kōtsū|宮崎交通|}} bus route == References == {{reflist}} == Further Reading == * {{Cite book|和書|title=大東亜戦争終結七十年記念誌 |publisher=宮崎県神道青年会 |url=https://www.m-shinsei.jp/kanri/wp-content/uploads/2022/11/70nen.pdf |date=2016-08-15}} * {{Cite journal |和書|author=森幸雄 |title=護国神社を事例とする、都市のシンボル的施設に対するイメージの変遷 |url=https://hdl.handle.net/10911/2440 |journal=Sociologica |volume=27 |issue=第1,2合併号 |publisher=[[Soka University]] |date=2003rd year 3月 |pages=25-45 |naid=110006608846}} == Related topics == * {{nihongo|Kawanami Gokoku Shrine|川南護国神社|1=Kawaminami Gokoku Shrine|}} - A Gokoku shrine established in Kawanami Town, Koyu District, Miyazaki Prefecture after World War II == External links == {{Commonscat|Miyazakiken-Gokoku-jinja}} * [https://miyazaki-gokoku.jp/ Miyazaki Gokoku Shrine] - Official website * {{Facebook|miyazakigokoku|宮﨑県護国神社}} {{citation needed|Miyazaki Gokoku Shrine}} {{Gokoku Shrines}} \ {{⚠️🚨COPIED from EverybodyWiki ❗❕⚠️😡😤Please respect Licence CC-BY-SA ❗}} {{Source Wikipedia}} {{draft categories| }} {{citation needed|Miyazaki Gokoku Shrine}} {{draft categories| [[Category:11]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Miyazaki Prefecture]] [[Category:Miyazaki, Miyazaki]] [[Category:Gokoku shrines]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Miyazaki]] [[Category:History of Miyazaki]] [[Category:Pages from everybodywiki under right title]] [[Category:Completion tier 2]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Miyazaki Gokoku Shrine]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Miyazaki Gokoku Shrine]][[Category:qq]] }} [[sn:Miyazaki Gokoku Shrine]] [[en:draft:Miyazaki Gokoku Shrine]] [[ja:宮崎縣護國神社]] [[en:Miyazaki Gokoku Shrine]]

It was then automatically transferred to everybodywiki and recovered from there. In the future it will be good to import the revisions. Check page history in case this page was moved, since the everybodywiki page may have been under a different title[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 20:10, 11 February 2025 (UTC)}} {{draft}} {{Promising draft|reason=the high amount of work put into it over a long time, see edit history}} {{Draft topics|east-asia}} {{AfC topic|geo}} {{draft|draft}} {{Infobox religious building|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | image = Haiden of Miyazaki Prefecture Gokoku Shrine.jpg | map_type = Japan | coordinates = | map_size = | map_alt = | map_relief = | map_caption = | location = | festival = | type = ''[[Gokoku shrine]]''
(Formerly ''[[Shokonsha]]'') | leadership = | website = | architecture_style = | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = }} {{ill|Miyazaki Gokoku Shrine|ja|宮崎縣護國神社}} is a [[Gokoku shrine]] located in [[Japan]], dedicated to over 40,000 natives of [[Miyazaki Prefecture]] who died in war. In comparison to other [[Gokoku shrines]] its building took longer because of a lack of well known [[military history]] of [[Miyazaki Prefecture]], since the prefecture was made during the abolition of the provincial system by combinging parts of many provinces without its own local identiy.https://www.mlit.go.jp/tagengo-db/common/001565649.pdf The shrine was only completed in 1955 after the end of the [[Occupation of Japan]]. There was a government coverup of the [[COVID-19]] infected governor going there.{{Cite web |title=Officials tried to hide shrine visits by infected Miyazaki chief {{!}} The Asahi Shimbun: Breaking News, Japan News and Analysis |url=https://www.asahi.com/ajw/articles/14808701 |access-date=2023-10-25 |website=The Asahi Shimbun |language=en}} It has its own [[Chinju no Mori]] with feral roosters living in it{{Cite web |title=The Miyazaki Shrine Forest - Part 3 - Miyazaki |url=https://en.japantravel.com/miyazaki/the-miyazaki-shrine-forest-part-3/5468 |access-date=2023-04-23 |website=JapanTravel |date=8 July 2013 |language=en}} Sand was dedicated to it{{Cite news |date=2023-08-15 |title=Iwo Jima sand returned 'home' to relatives of WWII Japanese soldiers via US researcher |language=en |work=Mainichi Daily News |url=https://mainichi.jp/english/articles/20230815/p2a/00m/0na/011000c |access-date=2023-10-25}} {{ill|Miyazaki Gokoku Shrine|ja|宮崎縣護國神社}} , alongside {{ill|Kumamoto Gokoku Shrine|ja|熊本県護国神社}} were completed after World War II, when the war ended and after the [[Ministry of home affairs|Ministry of Home Affairs]] was abolished, so they were not designated as [[Gokoku Shrines]] by the Minister of Home Affairs and are actually correctly designated as "equivalent to designated Gokoku-jinja Shrine". == See Also == * [[Controversies surrounding Yasukuni Shrine]] * [[Martyrs' shrines (China)|Martyr's Shrines]] * [[:en:Martial temple|Martial temple]] and [[:en:Wen Wu temple|Wen Wu temple]] * [[National Revolutionary Martyrs' Shrine]] * [[Eternal Spring Shrine]] * [[:en:Chinese Cultural Renaissance|Chinese Cultural Renaissance]] * [[Ancestral shrine]] * [[:en:Gallant Garden|Gallant Garden]] * [[Gokoku Shrines]] * [[:en:Tomb of the Unknown Soldier|Tomb of the Unknown Soldier]] * [[:en:Arlington National Cemetery|Arlington National Cemetery]] * [[:en:Valhalla|Valhalla]] (home to the souls of fallen warriors in Scandinavian mythology) * [[:en:Walhalla (memorial)|Walhalla Shrine]] (a hall of fame in Germany honoring "commendable and honorable Germans") * [[Eternal Spring Shrine]] * [[The common end of myriad good deeds]] * [[:en:Greek hero cult|Greek hero cult]] {{Citation needed|date=2017-08-28}} {{Infobox religious building|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = Following Japan’s defeat, the shrine construction was forcibly abandoned under the orders of the GHQ’s Miyazaki Civil Affairs Chief, Navy Lieutenant Masman. On June 13, 1954, it was decided to establish the shrine within the precincts of {{nihongo|Miyazaki Jingu|宮崎神宮|}}, and the shrine was officially created on March 10, 1955. Subsequently, it was registered as a religious corporation on December 24, 1955[https://www.m-shinsei.jp/m_shrine/宮崎県護國神社（みやざきけんごこくじんじゃ）/ Miyazaki Gokoku Shrine (宮崎県護國神社)] Miyazaki Shrine Introduction Site, Miyazaki Prefecture Shinto Youth Association, April 20, 2012. == Facilities == [[File:View in Miyazaki Prefecture Gokoku Shrine.jpg|thumb|250px|Memorial monument within Miyazaki Gokoku Shrine (March 2017)]] * Relics Hall * Memorial monument for the war dead from the {{ill|Bougainville Island|en|ブーゲンビル島}} campaign * Monument for war dead from the 1st Taiwan Infantry Regiment from Miyazaki Prefecture * Monument for the war dead from the {{ill|37th Division (Imperial Japanese Army)|en|第37師団 (日本軍)}} * Monument for the 3710th Unit * Memorial for the Five Kamikaze Heroes of Shikishima Unit, including {{nihongo|Yukio Seki|関行男|}}, {{nihongo|Iwao Nakano|中野磐雄|}}, {{nihongo|Nobuo Tani|谷暢夫|}}, {{nihongo|Hajime Nagamine|永峰肇|}}, and {{nihongo|Shigeo Oguro|大黒繁男|}} * Monument for the {{nihongo|72nd Infantry Regiment|歩兵第72連隊|}} * Statue of the Kamikaze Heroes File:Miyazakiken-Gokoku-jinja, Memorial Center 01.jpg|Relics Hall == Relation with Miyazaki Jingu == Initially built in the western forest of Miyazaki Jingu, the shrine was operated jointly with Miyazaki Jingu. However, due to the wishes of many citizens, including bereaved families, for independent management, dedicated staff were appointed in April 1972. == festivals == * Grand festival (April 10) ** Initially held on March 10 to commemorate the shrine’s founding and enshrinement, but moved to April 10 in 1978 to accommodate better attendance and the schedules of key officials[https://miyazakijingu.or.jp/publics/index/32/detail=1/b_id=128/r_id=340/ Memorial] Miyazaki Jingu Blog, April 11, 2018. * Mitama festival (August 14-15) * Daily Memorial Service (11:00 AM) == Nearby facilities == * {{ill|Miyazaki Jingū|ja|宮崎神宮}} == Access == * 10-minute walk from {{nihongo|Miyazaki Jingu Station|宮崎神宮駅|}} on the {{nihongo|Nippō Main Line|日豊本線|Nippō Honsen}} * 5-minute walk from the Miyazaki Jingu bus stop on the {{nihongo|Miyazaki Kōtsū|宮崎交通|}} bus route == References == {{reflist}} == Further Reading == * {{Cite book|和書|title=大東亜戦争終結七十年記念誌 |publisher=宮崎県神道青年会 |url=https://www.m-shinsei.jp/kanri/wp-content/uploads/2022/11/70nen.pdf |date=2016-08-15}} * {{Cite journal |和書|author=森幸雄 |title=護国神社を事例とする、都市のシンボル的施設に対するイメージの変遷 |url=https://hdl.handle.net/10911/2440 |journal=Sociologica |volume=27 |issue=第1,2合併号 |publisher=[[Soka University]] |date=2003rd year 3月 |pages=25-45 |naid=110006608846}} == Related topics == * {{nihongo|Kawanami Gokoku Shrine|川南護国神社|1=Kawaminami Gokoku Shrine|}} - (Formerly ''[[Shokonsha]]'') | leadership = | website = | architecture_style = | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = }} {{ill|Miyazaki Gokoku Shrine|ja|宮崎縣護國神社}} is a [[Gokoku shrine]] located in [[Japan]], dedicated to over 40,000 natives of [[Miyazaki Prefecture]] who died in war. In comparison to other [[Gokoku shrines]] its building took longer because of a lack of well known [[military history]] of [[Miyazaki Prefecture]], since the prefecture was made during the abolition of the provincial system by combinging parts of many provinces without its own local identiy.https://www.mlit.go.jp/tagengo-db/common/001565649.pdf The shrine was only completed in 1955 after the end of the [[Occupation of Japan]]. There was a government coverup of the [[COVID-19]] infected governor going there.{{Cite web |title=Officials tried to hide shrine visits by infected Miyazaki chief {{!}} The Asahi Shimbun: Breaking News, Japan News and Analysis |url=https://www.asahi.com/ajw/articles/14808701 |access-date=2023-10-25 |website=The Asahi Shimbun |language=en}} It has its own [[Chinju no Mori]] with feral roosters living in it{{Cite web |title=The Miyazaki Shrine Forest - Part 3 - Miyazaki |url=https://en.japantravel.com/miyazaki/the-miyazaki-shrine-forest-part-3/5468 |access-date=2023-04-23 |website=JapanTravel |date=8 July 2013 |language=en}} Sand was dedicated to it{{Cite news |date=2023-08-15 |title=Iwo Jima sand returned 'home' to relatives of WWII Japanese soldiers via US researcher |language=en |work=Mainichi Daily News |url=https://mainichi.jp/english/articles/20230815/p2a/00m/0na/011000c |access-date=2023-10-25}} {{ill|Miyazaki Gokoku Shrine|ja|宮崎縣護國神社}} , alongside {{ill|Kumamoto Gokoku Shrine|ja|熊本県護国神社}} were completed after World War II, when the war ended and after the [[Ministry of home affairs|Ministry of Home Affairs]] was abolished, so they were not designated as [[Gokoku Shrines]] by the Minister of Home Affairs and are actually correctly designated as "equivalent to designated Gokoku-jinja Shrine". == See Also == * [[Controversies surrounding Yasukuni Shrine]] * [[Martyrs' shrines (China)|Martyr's Shrines]] * [[:en:Martial temple|Martial temple]] and [[:en:Wen Wu temple|Wen Wu temple]] * [[National Revolutionary Martyrs' Shrine]] * * [[:en:Greek hero cult|Greek hero cult]] {{Citation needed|date=2017-08-28}} {{Infobox religious building|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Miyazaki Gokoku Shrine | image = [[File:Haiden of Miyazaki Prefecture Gokoku Shrine.jpg|250px|Miyazaki Gokoku Shrine]]
Haiden (March 2017) | map_type = Japan Miyazaki | latd = 31 | latm = 56 | lats = 13.67 | latNS = N | longd = 131 | longm = 25 | longs = 18.35 | longEW = E | coordinates_display = inline,title | coordinates_region = JP | location = 2-4-3 Jingu, Miyazaki City, Miyazaki Prefecture | deity = Spirits of those who served the nation and public from Miyazaki Prefecture, totaling 41,874 (as of March 2016)"70th Anniversary of the End of the Greater East Asia War" Memorial Book, p. 8 | founded = March 10, 1955 | annual_festival = April 10 | website = [https://miyazaki-gokoku.jp/ Miyazaki Gokoku Shrine] }} [[File:Miyazakiken-Gokoku-jinja, Mitama festival 01.jpg|thumb|250px|Lanterns for the Mitama festival in the shrine grounds]] {{nihongo|'''Miyazaki Gokoku Shrine'''|宮崎縣護國神社|Miyazaki-ken Gokoku Jinja}} is a {{ill|Gokoku shrine|en|護国神社}} located in {{ill|Miyazaki, Miyazaki|en|宮崎市}}. The shrine enshrines the spirits of 41,820 individuals who served the nation and public from Miyazaki Prefecture since the {{nihongo|Boshin War|戊辰戦争|Boshin sensō}}, along with 54 public servants who died in the line of duty (as of March 2016). == Overview == Most of the Gokoku shrines across Japan were originally established as {{ill|Shokonsha|en|招魂社}} during the {{nihongo|Meiji Restoration|明治維新|Meiji Ishin}}, with rituals for memorial services conducted before World War II. However, due to the fragmented state of Miyazaki Prefecture during the Meiji RestorationAt the time, the Miyazaki area ({{nihongo|Hyuga Province|日向国|Hyūga no kuni}}) was divided into various territories such as the {{nihongo|Bakufu|天領|}}, {{nihongo|Nobeoka Domain|延岡藩|}}, {{nihongo|Takanabe Domain|高鍋藩|}}, {{nihongo|Sadowara Domain|佐土原藩|}}, {{nihongo|Obi Domain|飫肥藩|}}, and {{nihongo|Satsuma Domain|薩摩藩|}}., there was no single Shokonsha to enshrine the spirits of those who died for the nation across the entire prefecture. Instead, small portable shrines were used for annual memorial services. Before World War II, there were seven Shokonsha shrines in Miyazaki Prefecture"Sociologica 27", pp. 36-37. They were all renamed Gokoku shrines on April 1, 1939, following an Interior Ministry order"Renaming Shokonsha to Gokoku Shrines" (Showa 14 Interior Ministry Ordinance No. 12) "Official Gazette" No. 3656, March 15, 1939, p. 510 ({{NDLJP|2960149/2}}). However, none of them were designated by the Interior Minister as official Gokoku shrines and were instead considered unofficial Gokoku shrines"Number of Gokoku Shrines" "Outline of Interior Ministry Affairs Showa 16", Interior Ministry, February 20, 1942, pp. 375-376 ({{NDLJP|1450040/571}}). {| class="wikitable" ! Shrine name (as of March 1942) ! location (then) ! location (now) ! Notes |- | Hirose Gokoku Shrine | Kitanaka District, Hirose Village | Sadowara Town, Miyazaki City | Originally Naka Shimoshima Shokonsha, founded by Sadowara Domain Lord {{nihongo|Shimazu Tadaoki|島津忠寛|}} |- | Fukushima Gokoku Shrine | Minaminaka District, Fukushima Town | Nishikata, Kushima City | Originally Nishikata Shokonsha, located on the rear mound of Fukushima Kofun No. 4 (Kenjōzuka) |- | Miyakonojo Gokoku Shrine | Miyamaru Town, Miyakonojo City | Komatsubara Town, Miyakonojo City (abolished) | Originally Miyamaru Shokonsha, dissolved as a religious corporation on October 2, 1956"Eighty Years of Miyazaki Prefecture Governance, Vol. 1", Miyazaki Prefecture, March 31, 1967, p. 883 ({{NDLJP|3030426/510}}) |- | Takaoka Gokoku Shrine | Higashimorokata District, Takaoka Town | Takaoka Town, Miyazaki City | Originally Morokata Uchiyama Shokonsha |- | Oyamada Gokoku Shrine (Muka Gokoku Shrine) | Higashimorokata District, Muka Village | Takaoka Town, Miyazaki City | Originally Morokata Muka Shokonsha, founded at Muka Castle site |- | Takanabe Gokoku Shrine | Koyu District, Takanabe Town | Same | Originally Takanabe Shokonsha, founded at Takanabe Castle site by Takanabe Domain |- | Aya Gokoku Shrine | Higashimorokata District, Aya VillageAya Town was established as a town on October 1, 1932, but the source follows the original designation. | Aya Town, Higashimorokata District | Privately established Shokonsha |} With the outbreak of the {{nihongo|Greater East Asia War|大東亜戦争|Daitōa sensō}} on December 8, 1941, the number of war dead increased significantly, deepening the public’s respect and gratitude for the spirits of the fallen. Consequently, the governor of Miyazaki Prefecture, {{nihongo|Nagafune Katsumi|長船克己|}}, and 58 representatives from various sectors formed the Miyazaki Prefecture Gokoku Shrine Construction Association. Although there were calls to establish the shrine in different parts of the prefecture, it was ultimately decided to build it in the prefectural capital, Miyazaki City, which lacked a designated Gokoku Shrine. On April 23, 1943, Interior Minister {{nihongo|Ando Kisaburo|安藤紀三郎|}} approved the establishment of the Miyazaki Gokoku Shrine to enshrine 3,358 spirits, with the groundbreaking ceremony held on May 10, 1943. Construction continued despite laborious efforts and material shortages, but the project was halted on May 31, 1945, due to intensified air raids. Following Japan’s defeat, the shrine construction was forcibly abandoned under the orders of the GHQ’s Miyazaki Civil Affairs Chief, Navy Lieutenant Masman. After the Treaty of San Francisco in 1952, efforts to rebuild the shrine resumed. Governor {{nihongo|Tanaka Nagashige|田中長茂|}} led the reorganization of the Miyazaki Prefecture Gokoku Shrine Rebuilding Association, which secured public donations to fund the project. On June 13, 1954, it was decided to establish the shrine within the precincts of {{nihongo|Miyazaki Jingu|宮崎神宮|}}, and the shrine was officially created on March 10, 1955. Subsequently, it was registered as a religious corporation on December 24, 1955[https://www.m-shinsei.jp/m_shrine/宮崎県護國神社（みやざきけんごこくじんじゃ）/ Miyazaki Gokoku Shrine (宮崎県護國神社)] Miyazaki Shrine Introduction Site, Miyazaki Prefecture Shinto Youth Association, April 20, 2012. == Facilities == [[File:View in Miyazaki Prefecture Gokoku Shrine.jpg|thumb|250px|Memorial monument within Miyazaki Gokoku Shrine (March 2017)]] * Relics Hall * Memorial monument for the war dead from the {{ill|Bougainville Island|en|ブーゲンビル島}} campaign * Monument for war dead from the 1st Taiwan Infantry Regiment from Miyazaki Prefecture * Monument for the war dead from the {{ill|37th Division (Imperial Japanese Army)|en|第37師団 (日本軍)}} * Monument for the 3710th Unit * Memorial for the Five Kamikaze Heroes of Shikishima Unit, including {{nihongo|Yukio Seki|関行男|}}, {{nihongo|Iwao Nakano|中野磐雄|}}, {{nihongo|Nobuo Tani|谷暢夫|}}, {{nihongo|Hajime Nagamine|永峰肇|}}, and {{nihongo|Shigeo Oguro|大黒繁男|}} * Monument for the {{nihongo|72nd Infantry Regiment|歩兵第72連隊|}} * Statue of the Kamikaze Heroes File:Miyazakiken-Gokoku-jinja, Memorial Center 01.jpg|Relics Hall == Relation with Miyazaki Jingu == Initially built in the western forest of Miyazaki Jingu, the shrine was operated jointly with Miyazaki Jingu. However, due to the wishes of many citizens, including bereaved families, for independent management, dedicated staff were appointed in April 1972. == festivals == * Grand festival (April 10) ** Initially held on March 10 to commemorate the shrine’s founding and enshrinement, but moved to April 10 in 1978 to accommodate better attendance and the schedules of key officials[https://miyazakijingu.or.jp/publics/index/32/detail=1/b_id=128/r_id=340/ Memorial] Miyazaki Jingu Blog, April 11, 2018. * Mitama festival (August 14-15) * Daily Memorial Service (11:00 AM) == Nearby facilities == * {{ill|Miyazaki Jingū|ja|宮崎神宮}} == Access == * 10-minute walk from {{nihongo|Miyazaki Jingu Station|宮崎神宮駅|}} on the {{nihongo|Nippō Main Line|日豊本線|Nippō Honsen}} * 5-minute walk from the Miyazaki Jingu bus stop on the {{nihongo|Miyazaki Kōtsū|宮崎交通|}} bus route == References == {{reflist}} == Further Reading == * {{Cite book|和書|title=大東亜戦争終結七十年記念誌 |publisher=宮崎県神道青年会 |url=https://www.m-shinsei.jp/kanri/wp-content/uploads/2022/11/70nen.pdf |date=2016-08-15}} * {{Cite journal |和書|author=森幸雄 |title=護国神社を事例とする、都市のシンボル的施設に対するイメージの変遷 |url=https://hdl.handle.net/10911/2440 |journal=Sociologica |volume=27 |issue=第1,2合併号 |publisher=[[Soka University]] |date=2003rd year 3月 |pages=25-45 |naid=110006608846}} == Related topics == * {{nihongo|Kawanami Gokoku Shrine|川南護国神社|1=Kawaminami Gokoku Shrine|}} - A Gokoku shrine established in Kawanami Town, Koyu District, Miyazaki Prefecture after World War II == External links == {{Commonscat|Miyazakiken-Gokoku-jinja}} * [https://miyazaki-gokoku.jp/ Miyazaki Gokoku Shrine] - Official website * {{Facebook|miyazakigokoku|宮﨑県護国神社}} {{citation needed|Miyazaki Gokoku Shrine}} {{Gokoku Shrines}} \ {{⚠️🚨COPIED from EverybodyWiki ❗❕⚠️😡😤Please respect Licence CC-BY-SA ❗}} {{Source Wikipedia}} {{draft categories| }} {{citation needed|Miyazaki Gokoku Shrine}} {{draft categories| [[Category:11]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Miyazaki Prefecture]] [[Category:Miyazaki, Miyazaki]] [[Category:Gokoku shrines]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Miyazaki]] [[Category:History of Miyazaki]] [[Category:Pages from everybodywiki under right title]] [[Category:Completion tier 2]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Miyazaki Gokoku Shrine]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Miyazaki Gokoku Shrine]][[Category:qq]] }} [[sn:Miyazaki Gokoku Shrine]] [[en:draft:Miyazaki Gokoku Shrine]] [[ja:宮崎縣護國神社]] [[en:Miyazaki Gokoku Shrine]]

Governor {{nihongo|Tanaka Nagashige|田中長茂|}} led the reorganization of the Miyazaki Prefecture Gokoku Shrine Rebuilding Association, which secured public donations to fund the project. On June 13, 1954, it was decided to establish the shrine within the precincts of {{nihongo|Miyazaki Jingu|宮崎神宮|}}, and the shrine was officially created on March 10, 1955. Subsequently, it was registered as a religious corporation on December 24, 1955[https://www.m-shinsei.jp/m_shrine/宮崎県護國神社（みやざきけんごこくじんじゃ）/ Miyazaki Gokoku Shrine (宮崎県護國神社)] Miyazaki Shrine Introduction Site, Miyazaki Prefecture Shinto Youth Association, April 20, 2012. == Facilities == [[File:View in Miyazaki Prefecture Gokoku Shrine.jpg|thumb|250px|Memorial monument within Miyazaki Gokoku Shrine (March 2017)]] * Relics Hall * Memorial monument for the war dead from the {{ill|Bougainville Island|en|ブーゲンビル島}} campaign * Monument for war dead from the 1st Taiwan Infantry Regiment from Miyazaki Prefecture * Monument for the war dead from the {{ill|37th Division (Imperial Japanese Army)|en|第37師団 (日本軍)}} * Monument for the 3710th Unit * Memorial for the Five Kamikaze Heroes of Shikishima Unit, including {{nihongo|Yukio Seki|関行男|}}, {{nihongo|Iwao Nakano|中野磐雄|}}, {{nihongo|Nobuo Tani|谷暢夫|}}, {{nihongo|Hajime Nagamine|永峰肇|}}, and {{nihongo|Shigeo Oguro|大黒繁男|}} * Monument for the {{nihongo|72nd Infantry Regiment|歩兵第72連隊|}} * Statue of the Kamikaze Heroes File:Miyazakiken-Gokoku-jinja, Memorial Center 01.jpg|Relics Hall == Relation with Miyazaki Jingu == Initially built in the western forest of Miyazaki Jingu, the shrine was operated jointly with Miyazaki Jingu. However, due to the wishes of many citizens, including bereaved families, for independent management, dedicated staff were appointed in April 1972. == festivals == * Grand festival (April 10) ** Initially held on March 10 to commemorate the shrine’s founding and enshrinement, but moved to April 10 in 1978 to accommodate better attendance and the schedules of key officials[https://miyazakijingu.or.jp/publics/index/32/detail=1/b_id=128/r_id=340/ Memorial] Miyazaki Jingu Blog, April 11, 2018. * Mitama festival (August 14-15) * Daily Memorial Service (11:00 AM) == Nearby facilities == * {{ill|Miyazaki Jingū|ja|宮崎神宮}} == Access == * 10-minute walk from {{nihongo|Miyazaki Jingu Station|宮崎神宮駅|}} on the {{nihongo|Nippō Main Line|日豊本線|Nippō Honsen}} * 5-minute walk from the Miyazaki Jingu bus stop on the {{nihongo|Miyazaki Kōtsū|宮崎交通|}} bus route == References == {{reflist}} == Further Reading == * {{Cite book|和書|title=大東亜戦争終結七十年記念誌 |publisher=宮崎県神道青年会 |url=https://www.m-shinsei.jp/kanri/wp-content/uploads/2022/11/70nen.pdf |date=2016-08-15}} * {{Cite journal |和書|author=森幸雄 |title=護国神社を事例とする、都市のシンボル的施設に対するイメージの変遷 |url=https://hdl.handle.net/10911/2440 |journal=Sociologica |volume=27 |issue=第1,2合併号 |publisher=[[Soka University]] |date=2003rd year 3月 |pages=25-45 |naid=110006608846}} == Related topics == * {{nihongo|Kawanami Gokoku Shrine|川南護国神社|1=Kawaminami Gokoku Shrine|}} - A Gokoku shrine established in Kawanami Town, Koyu District, Miyazaki Prefecture after World War II == External links == {{Commonscat|Miyazakiken-Gokoku-jinja}} * [https://miyazaki-gokoku.jp/ Miyazaki Gokoku Shrine] - Official website * {{Facebook|miyazakigokoku|宮﨑県護国神社}} {{citation needed|Miyazaki Gokoku Shrine}} {{Gokoku Shrines}} \ {{⚠️🚨COPIED from EverybodyWiki ❗❕⚠️😡😤Please respect Licence CC-BY-SA ❗}} {{Source Wikipedia}} {{draft categories| }} {{citation needed|Miyazaki Gokoku Shrine}} {{draft categories| [[Category:11]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Miyazaki Prefecture]] [[Category:Miyazaki, Miyazaki]] [[Category:Gokoku shrines]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Miyazaki]] [[Category:History of Miyazaki]] [[Category:Pages from everybodywiki under right title]] [[Category:Completion tier 2]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Miyazaki Gokoku Shrine]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Miyazaki Gokoku Shrine]][[Category:qq]] }} [[sn:Miyazaki Gokoku Shrine]] [[en:draft:Miyazaki Gokoku Shrine]] [[ja:宮崎縣護國神社]] [[en:Miyazaki Gokoku Shrine]]

revision.comment: 10 revisions imported: Bot: import full history from ja:宮崎縣護國神社 up to rev 102022255
revision.text:
{{神社 | 名称 = 宮崎縣護國神社 | 画像 = [[File:Haiden of Miyazaki Prefecture Gokoku Shrine.jpg|250px|宮崎縣護國神社]]
拝殿（2017年3月） | 所在地 = [[宮崎県]][[宮崎市]][[神宮 (宮崎市)|神宮二丁目]]4−3 | 位置 = {{Coord|31|56|13.6|N|131|25|18.3|E|display=inline,title}} | 祭神 = [[宮崎県]]出身の[[国家]][[公共]]に尽くした人の神霊及び公務[[殉職|殉職者]]の[[御霊]]41,874柱（[[2016年]]3月現在）『大東亜戦争終結七十年記念誌』、8頁 | 神体 = | 社格 = 旧[[内務大臣 (日本)|内務大臣]][[護国神社#指定護国神社|指定護国神社]]相当内務大臣から県名を冠した「宮崎縣護國神社」創立を許可されていたが（指定護国神社以外は社名に道府県名を冠することが出来ない）、造営中に終戦を迎え、[[内務省 (日本)|内務省]]廃止後の第二次世界大戦後に竣工したため、内務大臣指定は行われてない。 | 創建 = [[1955年]]（[[昭和]]30年）3月10日 | 本殿 = | 別名 = | 札所等 = | 例祭 = 例大祭：4月10日 | 神事 = | 地図 = {{Location map|Japan Miyazaki|label=宮崎縣
護國神社|coordinates= {{coord2|31|56|13.6|N|131|25|18.3|E}}|position=bottom|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=11|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|coord={{coord|31|56|13.6|N|131|25|18.3|E}}}} }} [[File:Miyazakiken-Gokoku-jinja, Mitama Festival 01.jpg|thumb|250px|みたま祭の提灯が掛かる境内]] '''宮崎縣護國神社'''（みやざきけんごこくじんじゃ）は、[[宮崎県]][[宮崎市]]にある[[神社]]（[[護国神社]]）である。[[祭神]]として[[神社建築#本殿|本殿]]に[[戊辰戦争|戊辰の役]]以降の[[国家]][[公共]]に尽くした人の神霊41,820柱、相殿に公務[[殉職|殉職者]]の[[御霊]]54柱を祀る（[[2016年]]3月現在）。 == 概要 == 全国道府県の護国神社の殆どは[[明治維新]]の当時に[[招魂社]]として創建され、[[第二次世界大戦]]前より慰霊の祭儀が行われていた。しかし、[[宮崎県]]は明治維新の当時、小藩分立の状態であった等の事情もあり当時の宮崎県地域（[[日向国]]）は[[天領|幕府領]]、[[延岡藩]]、[[高鍋藩]]、[[佐土原藩]]、[[飫肥藩]]、[[薩摩藩]]等が分立していた。、県内全域の御霊を祀る招魂社は創立されていなかった。そこで宮崎県は移設のできる小さな社殿を造り、毎年祭場を設営して宮崎県下全戦歿者の招魂祭を執り行っていた。 第二次世界大戦以前、宮崎県内には7つの招魂社があり『Sociologica 27』、36-37頁、[[1939年]]（[[昭和]]14年）3月15日[[公布]]、同4月1日[[施行]]された「招魂社ヲ護國神社ト改称スルノ件」（昭和14年[[内務省 (日本)|内務]][[省令]]第12號）によって一斉に護国神社に改称したが「招魂社ヲ護國神社ト改稱」（昭和14年3月14日内務省令第12號）『官報』第3656號、1939年（昭和14年）3月15日、510頁（{{NDLJP|2960149/2}}）、いずれも[[内務大臣 (日本)|内務大臣]]が指定した府県社に相当する'''指定護国神社'''ではなく、それ以外の村社に相当する'''指定外護国神社'''だった「護國神社數」『昭和16年 内務省事務概要』、内務省、1942年2月20日、375-376頁（{{NDLJP|1450040/571}}）。以下はその一覧である『昭和18年版 陸海軍軍事年鑑』、軍人会館図書部、1943年6月5日、47頁（{{NDLJP|1451458/47}}）。 {| class="wikitable" ! 社名（昭和17年3月末当時） ! 所在地（当時） ! 所在地（現在） ! 備考 |- | 廣瀬護國神社 | 宮崎県[[北那珂郡]]廣瀬村 | 宮崎県宮崎市[[佐土原町]] | 官祭招魂社。那珂下田嶋招魂社から改称。[[佐土原藩]]知事[[島津忠寛]]が創建 |- | 福島護國神社 | 宮崎県[[南那珂郡]]福島町大字西方 | 宮崎県[[串間市]]西方 | 官祭招魂社。西方招魂社から改称。福島古墳4号（剣城塚）の後円部に創建 |- | 都城護國神社 | 宮崎県[[都城市]]宮丸町 | 宮崎県都城市小松原町（廃絶） | 官祭招魂社。宮丸招魂社から改称。1956年（昭和31年）10月2日に宗教法人を解散『宮崎県政八十年史 上巻』、宮崎県、1967年3月31日、883頁（{{NDLJP|3030426/510}}） |- | 高岡護國神社 | 宮崎県[[東諸県郡|東諸縣郡]][[高岡町 (宮崎県)|高岡町]] | 宮崎県宮崎市高岡町 | 官祭招魂社。諸縣内山招魂社から改称 |- | 小山田護國神社（穆佐護國神社） | 宮崎県東諸縣郡穆佐村 | 宮崎県宮崎市[[高岡町 (宮崎県)|高岡町]] | 官祭招魂社。諸縣穆佐招魂社から改称。[[穆佐城]]跡に創建 |- | 高鍋護國神社 | 宮崎県[[児湯郡]][[高鍋町]] | 同左 | 官祭招魂社。高鍋招魂社から改称。[[高鍋藩]]が[[高鍋城]]跡に創建 |- | 綾護國神社 | 宮崎県[[東諸県郡]]綾村綾町は1932年（昭和7年）10月1日に町制施行しているが、出典表記に従う。 | 宮崎県[[東諸県郡]][[綾町]] | 私祭招魂社。 |} [[1941年]]（昭和16年）12月8日の[[大東亜戦争]]（[[太平洋戦争]]）勃発に伴い戦歿者の数は著しく増加し、護国の英霊に対する県民の感謝と敬仰の念が次第に深まった。そこで県民の要望に応えるため、[[宮崎県知事一覧|宮崎県知事]]・[[長船克巳]]をはじめ各界の代表者58名が設立者となって、宮崎縣護國神社建設奉賛会が組織された。宮崎県内各地から指定護国神社の誘致の声が上がり、特に[[宮崎連隊区|宮崎聯隊区]]が置かれ、[[軍郷|軍都]]と呼ばれた[[都城市]]は誘致に熱心だったが、最終的に県の中枢（[[都道府県庁所在地|県庁所在地]]）で護国神社を持たない宮崎市に決まり、鎮座地を宮崎市下北方町の高台（現・[[宮崎市立大宮中学校]]）に決定した。[[1943年]]（昭和18年）4月23日、内務大臣[[安藤紀三郎]]から、蓑毛次右衞直行外3,358柱を祭神とする「宮崎縣護國神社創立」の許可を得て「宮崎縣護國神社創立」（昭和17年4月28日内務省告示第234號）『官報』第4885號、1943年（昭和18年）4月28日、776頁（{{NDLJP|2961390/5}}）、同年5月10日に[[地鎮祭]]を斎行、11月20日に起工式が行われて造営に着手した。勤労奉仕による整地作業が終わり、建築用材及び建設資金を集めたが、連日の[[日本本土空襲|空襲]]により[[1945年]]（昭和20年）5月31日を以って建設工事は中止となり、同年8月15日の[[終戦の日]]を迎え、日本は[[連合国 (第二次世界大戦)|連合国]]の占領下に置かれた。第二次世界大戦後、宮崎縣護國神社建設奉賛会は解散を命じられ、当時のGHQ（[[連合国軍最高司令官総司令部]]）宮崎軍民政部長官マスマン[[アメリカ海軍|海軍]][[少佐]]の厳命により建設放棄を余儀なくされた。 [[1952年]]（昭和27年）4月の[[日本国との平和条約|サンフランシスコ講和条約]]発効後、護国神社再建の要望が起こり、[[1953年]]（昭和28年）4月に宮崎県知事・[[田中長茂]]を総裁、[[宮崎県議会]]議長・日高弥一を会長とする宮崎県護国神社再建奉賛会を組織し、県民の[[寄付|浄財]]を募って再建に着手した。[[1954年]]（昭和29年）6月13日、役員会で[[宮崎神宮]]の境内神社として設立することを議決し、同年6月30日付を以って[[神社本庁]]統理・[[鷹司信輔]]の承認を受け、宮崎神宮境内神社として創立した。[[1955年]]（昭和30年）3月10日竣工、同日鎮座祭が斎行された。その後、本神社を[[宗教法人法]]による[[宗教法人]]として設立する議運が起こり、同年11月26日に神社本庁統理の承認、翌12月21日に宮崎県知事の認証を受け、1955年（昭和30年）12月24日に宗教法人設立の[[登記]]を完了して今日に至る[https://www.m-shinsei.jp/m_shrine/宮崎県護國神社（みやざきけんごこくじんじゃ）/ 宮巡 ～神主さんが作る宮崎県の神社紹介サイト～ 宮崎県護國神社（みやざきけんごこくじんじゃ）]、宮崎県神道青年会、2012年4月20日。 == 施設 == [[File:View in Miyazaki Prefecture Gokoku Shrine.jpg|thumb|250px|宮崎縣護國神社境内にある[[慰霊碑]]（2017年3月）]] * 遺品館 * [[ブーゲンビル島|ブーゲンビル]]戦歿者[[慰霊碑]] * [[歩兵連隊#台湾歩兵連隊|臺湾歩兵第一聯隊]]宮崎県出身戦歿者慰霊之碑 * [[第37師団 (日本軍)|第三十七師団]]戦歿者慰霊碑 * 広三七一〇部隊記念碑 * 敷島隊五軍神慰霊碑 - [[神風特別攻撃隊]]・敷島隊として出撃し、後に[[軍神]]となった[[関行男]]、[[中野磐雄]]、[[谷暢夫]]、[[永峰肇]]、[[大黒繁男]]の慰霊碑 * [[歩兵第72連隊|歩兵第七十二連隊]]慰霊碑 * [[特攻勇士の像|特攻勇士之像]] Miyazakiken-Gokoku-jinja, Memorial Center 01.jpg|遺品館 == 宮崎神宮との関係 == 宮崎神宮境内の西の森に創建された当初は、宮崎神宮と一体で運営がなされていたが、戦歿者[[遺族]]を始めとする多くの県民から独立神社に相応しい運営をとの願いにより、[[1972年]]（昭和47年）4月から専任職員を置くようになった。 == 祭事 == * [[例祭|例大祭]]（4月10日） ** 神社が創建され、合祀鎮座祭が斎行された3月10日を例祭日と定めて[[祭祀 (神道)|祭祀]]を行ってきたが、宮崎県戦没者等慰霊奉賛会会長（宮崎県議会議長）より、3月10日は宮崎県議会の2月定例会開催中につき、慰霊奉賛会総裁（宮崎県知事）、慰霊奉賛会会長（宮崎県議会議長）の参列が困難であるとのことから、例祭日変更に関する申し出があった。また、県下の戦歿者遺族にとっても、時期的に肌寒いため参集が困難であるという事情も考慮して、[[1978年]]（昭和53年）2月18日に神社本庁統理・[[徳川宗敬]]の承認を受けて例祭日は4月10日に改められた[https://miyazakijingu.or.jp/publics/index/32/detail=1/b_id=128/r_id=340/ 慰霊]、宮崎神宮ブログ、2018年4月11日。 * みたま祭（8月14日・15日） * 命日祭（毎日午前11時） == 近隣施設 == * [[宮崎神宮]] == 交通 == * JR[[日豊本線]][[宮崎神宮駅]]から徒歩10分。 * [[宮崎交通]]宮崎神宮[[バス停留所|バス停]]から徒歩5分。 == 脚注 == {{Reflist|2}} == 参考資料 == * {{Cite book|和書|title=大東亜戦争終結七十年記念誌 |publisher=宮崎県神道青年会 |url=https://www.m-shinsei.jp/kanri/wp-content/uploads/2022/11/70nen.pdf |date=2016-08-15}} * {{Cite journal |和書|author=森幸雄 |title=護国神社を事例とする、都市のシンボル的施設に対するイメージの変遷 |url=https://hdl.handle.net/10911/2440 |journal=Sociologica |volume=27 |issue=第1,2合併号 |publisher=[[創価大学]] |date=2003年3月 |pages=25-45 |naid=110006608846}} == 関連項目 == * [[川南護国神社]] - 第二次世界大戦後、[[宮崎県]][[児湯郡]][[川南町]]に創建された護国神社 == 外部リンク == {{Commonscat|Miyazakiken-Gokoku-jinja}} * [https://miyazaki-gokoku.jp/ 宮崎縣護國神社] - 宮崎県護国神社公式ホームページ * {{Facebook|miyazakigokoku|宮﨑県護国神社}} {{DEFAULTSORT:みやさきここくしんしや}} [[Category:宮崎県の神社]] [[Category:宮崎市の歴史]] [[Category:護国神社]] [[Category:宮崎市の建築物]]

拝殿（2017年3月） | 所在地 = [[宮崎県]][[宮崎市]][[神宮 (宮崎市)|神宮二丁目]]4−3 | 位置 = {{Coord|31|56|13.6|N|131|25|18.3|E|display=inline,title}} | 祭神 = [[宮崎県]]出身の[[国家]][[公共]]に尽くした人の神霊及び公務[[殉職|殉職者]]の[[御霊]]41,874柱（[[2016年]]3月現在）『大東亜戦争終結七十年記念誌』、8頁 | 神体 = | 社格 = 旧[[内務大臣 (日本)|内務大臣]][[護国神社#指定護国神社|指定護国神社]]相当内務大臣から県名を冠した「宮崎縣護國神社」創立を許可されていたが（指定護国神社以外は社名に道府県名を冠することが出来ない）、造営中に終戦を迎え、[[内務省 (日本)|内務省]]廃止後の第二次世界大戦後に竣工したため、内務大臣指定は行われてない。 | 創建 = [[1955年]]（[[昭和]]30年）3月10日 | 本殿 = | 別名 = | 札所等 = | 例祭 = 例大祭：4月10日 | 神事 = | 地図 = {{Location map|Japan Miyazaki|label=宮崎縣
護國神社|coordinates= {{coord2|31|56|13.6|N|131|25|18.3|E}}|position=bottom|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=11|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|coord={{coord|31|56|13.6|N|131|25|18.3|E}}}} }} [[File:Miyazakiken-Gokoku-jinja, Mitama Festival 01.jpg|thumb|250px|みたま祭の提灯が掛かる境内]] '''宮崎縣護國神社'''（みやざきけんごこくじんじゃ）は、[[宮崎県]][[宮崎市]]にある[[神社]]（[[護国神社]]）である。[[祭神]]として[[神社建築#本殿|本殿]]に[[戊辰戦争|戊辰の役]]以降の[[国家]][[公共]]に尽くした人の神霊41,820柱、相殿に公務[[殉職|殉職者]]の[[御霊]]54柱を祀る（[[2016年]]3月現在）。 == 概要 == 全国道府県の護国神社の殆どは[[明治維新]]の当時に[[招魂社]]として創建され、[[第二次世界大戦]]前より慰霊の祭儀が行われていた。しかし、[[宮崎県]]は明治維新の当時、小藩分立の状態であった等の事情もあり当時の宮崎県地域（[[日向国]]）は[[天領|幕府領]]、[[延岡藩]]、[[高鍋藩]]、[[佐土原藩]]、[[飫肥藩]]、[[薩摩藩]]等が分立していた。、県内全域の御霊を祀る招魂社は創立されていなかった。そこで宮崎県は移設のできる小さな社殿を造り、毎年祭場を設営して宮崎県下全戦歿者の招魂祭を執り行っていた。 第二次世界大戦以前、宮崎県内には7つの招魂社があり『Sociologica 27』、36-37頁、[[1939年]]（[[昭和]]14年）3月15日[[公布]]、同4月1日[[施行]]された「招魂社ヲ護國神社ト改称スルノ件」（昭和14年[[内務省 (日本)|内務]][[省令]]第12號）によって一斉に護国神社に改称したが「招魂社ヲ護國神社ト改稱」（昭和14年3月14日内務省令第12號）『官報』第3656號、1939年（昭和14年）3月15日、510頁（{{NDLJP|2960149/2}}）、いずれも[[内務大臣 (日本)|内務大臣]]が指定した府県社に相当する'''指定護国神社'''ではなく、それ以外の村社に相当する'''指定外護国神社'''だった「護國神社數」『昭和16年 内務省事務概要』、内務省、1942年2月20日、375-376頁（{{NDLJP|1450040/571}}）。以下はその一覧である『昭和18年版 陸海軍軍事年鑑』、軍人会館図書部、1943年6月5日、47頁（{{NDLJP|1451458/47}}）。 {| class="wikitable" ! 社名（昭和17年3月末当時） ! 所在地（当時） ! 所在地（現在） ! 備考 |- | 廣瀬護國神社 | 宮崎県[[北那珂郡]]廣瀬村 | 宮崎県宮崎市[[佐土原町]] | 官祭招魂社。那珂下田嶋招魂社から改称。[[佐土原藩]]知事[[島津忠寛]]が創建 |- | 福島護國神社 | 宮崎県[[南那珂郡]]福島町大字西方 | 宮崎県[[串間市]]西方 | 官祭招魂社。西方招魂社から改称。福島古墳4号（剣城塚）の後円部に創建 |- | 都城護國神社 | 宮崎県[[都城市]]宮丸町 | 宮崎県都城市小松原町（廃絶） | 官祭招魂社。宮丸招魂社から改称。1956年（昭和31年）10月2日に宗教法人を解散『宮崎県政八十年史 上巻』、宮崎県、1967年3月31日、883頁（{{NDLJP|3030426/510}}） |- | 高岡護國神社 | 宮崎県[[東諸県郡|東諸縣郡]][[高岡町 (宮崎県)|高岡町]] | 宮崎県宮崎市高岡町 | 官祭招魂社。諸縣内山招魂社から改称 |- | 小山田護國神社（穆佐護國神社） | 宮崎県東諸縣郡穆佐村 | 宮崎県宮崎市[[高岡町 (宮崎県)|高岡町]] | 官祭招魂社。諸縣穆佐招魂社から改称。[[穆佐城]]跡に創建 |- | 高鍋護國神社 | 宮崎県[[児湯郡]][[高鍋町]] | 同左 | 官祭招魂社。高鍋招魂社から改称。[[高鍋藩]]が[[高鍋城]]跡に創建 |- | 綾護國神社 | 宮崎県[[東諸県郡]]綾村綾町は1932年（昭和7年）10月1日に町制施行しているが、出典表記に従う。 | 宮崎県[[東諸県郡]][[綾町]] | 私祭招魂社。 |} [[1941年]]（昭和16年）12月8日の[[大東亜戦争]]（[[太平洋戦争]]）勃発に伴い戦歿者の数は著しく増加し、護国の英霊に対する県民の感謝と敬仰の念が次第に深まった。そこで県民の要望に応えるため、[[宮崎県知事一覧|宮崎県知事]]・[[長船克巳]]をはじめ各界の代表者58名が設立者となって、宮崎縣護國神社建設奉賛会が組織された。宮崎県内各地から指定護国神社の誘致の声が上がり、特に[[宮崎連隊区|宮崎聯隊区]]が置かれ、[[軍郷|軍都]]と呼ばれた[[都城市]]は誘致に熱心だったが、最終的に県の中枢（[[都道府県庁所在地|県庁所在地]]）で護国神社を持たない宮崎市に決まり、鎮座地を宮崎市下北方町の高台（現・[[宮崎市立大宮中学校]]）に決定した。[[1943年]]（昭和18年）4月23日、内務大臣[[安藤紀三郎]]から、蓑毛次右衞直行外3,358柱を祭神とする「宮崎縣護國神社創立」の許可を得て「宮崎縣護國神社創立」（昭和17年4月28日内務省告示第234號）『官報』第4885號、1943年（昭和18年）4月28日、776頁（{{NDLJP|2961390/5}}）、同年5月10日に[[地鎮祭]]を斎行、11月20日に起工式が行われて造営に着手した。勤労奉仕による整地作業が終わり、建築用材及び建設資金を集めたが、連日の[[日本本土空襲|空襲]]により[[1945年]]（昭和20年）5月31日を以って建設工事は中止となり、同年8月15日の[[終戦の日]]を迎え、日本は[[連合国 (第二次世界大戦)|連合国]]の占領下に置かれた。第二次世界大戦後、宮崎縣護國神社建設奉賛会は解散を命じられ、当時のGHQ（[[連合国軍最高司令官総司令部]]）宮崎軍民政部長官マスマン[[アメリカ海軍|海軍]][[少佐]]の厳命により建設放棄を余儀なくされた。 [[1952年]]（昭和27年）4月の[[日本国との平和条約|サンフランシスコ講和条約]]発効後、護国神社再建の要望が起こり、[[1953年]]（昭和28年）4月に宮崎県知事・[[田中長茂]]を総裁、[[宮崎県議会]]議長・日高弥一を会長とする宮崎県護国神社再建奉賛会を組織し、県民の[[寄付|浄財]]を募って再建に着手した。[[1954年]]（昭和29年）6月13日、役員会で[[宮崎神宮]]の境内神社として設立することを議決し、同年6月30日付を以って[[神社本庁]]統理・[[鷹司信輔]]の承認を受け、宮崎神宮境内神社として創立した。[[1955年]]（昭和30年）3月10日竣工、同日鎮座祭が斎行された。その後、本神社を[[宗教法人法]]による[[宗教法人]]として設立する議運が起こり、同年11月26日に神社本庁統理の承認、翌12月21日に宮崎県知事の認証を受け、1955年（昭和30年）12月24日に宗教法人設立の[[登記]]を完了して今日に至る[https://www.m-shinsei.jp/m_shrine/宮崎県護國神社（みやざきけんごこくじんじゃ）/ 宮巡 ～神主さんが作る宮崎県の神社紹介サイト～ 宮崎県護國神社（みやざきけんごこくじんじゃ）]、宮崎県神道青年会、2012年4月20日。 == 施設 == [[File:View in Miyazaki Prefecture Gokoku Shrine.jpg|thumb|250px|宮崎縣護國神社境内にある[[慰霊碑]]（2017年3月）]] * 遺品館 * [[ブーゲンビル島|ブーゲンビル]]戦歿者[[慰霊碑]] * [[歩兵連隊#台湾歩兵連隊|臺湾歩兵第一聯隊]]宮崎県出身戦歿者慰霊之碑 * [[第37師団 (日本軍)|第三十七師団]]戦歿者慰霊碑 * 広三七一〇部隊記念碑 * 敷島隊五軍神慰霊碑 - [[神風特別攻撃隊]]・敷島隊として出撃し、後に[[軍神]]となった[[関行男]]、[[中野磐雄]]、[[谷暢夫]]、[[永峰肇]]、[[大黒繁男]]の慰霊碑 * [[歩兵第72連隊|歩兵第七十二連隊]]慰霊碑 * [[特攻勇士の像|特攻勇士之像]] Miyazakiken-Gokoku-jinja, Memorial Center 01.jpg|遺品館 == 宮崎神宮との関係 == 宮崎神宮境内の西の森に創建された当初は、宮崎神宮と一体で運営がなされていたが、戦歿者[[遺族]]を始めとする多くの県民から独立神社に相応しい運営をとの願いにより、[[1972年]]（昭和47年）4月から専任職員を置くようになった。 == 祭事 == * [[例祭|例大祭]]（4月10日） ** 神社が創建され、合祀鎮座祭が斎行された3月10日を例祭日と定めて[[祭祀 (神道)|祭祀]]を行ってきたが、宮崎県戦没者等慰霊奉賛会会長（宮崎県議会議長）より、3月10日は宮崎県議会の2月定例会開催中につき、慰霊奉賛会総裁（宮崎県知事）、慰霊奉賛会会長（宮崎県議会議長）の参列が困難であるとのことから、例祭日変更に関する申し出があった。また、県下の戦歿者遺族にとっても、時期的に肌寒いため参集が困難であるという事情も考慮して、[[1978年]]（昭和53年）2月18日に神社本庁統理・[[徳川宗敬]]の承認を受けて例祭日は4月10日に改められた[https://miyazakijingu.or.jp/publics/index/32/detail=1/b_id=128/r_id=340/ 慰霊]、宮崎神宮ブログ、2018年4月11日。 * みたま祭（8月14日・15日） * 命日祭（毎日午前11時） == 近隣施設 == * [[宮崎神宮]] == 交通 == * JR[[日豊本線]][[宮崎神宮駅]]から徒歩10分。 * [[宮崎交通]]宮崎神宮[[バス停留所|バス停]]から徒歩5分。 == 脚注 == {{Reflist|2}} == 参考資料 == * {{Cite (Formerly ''[[Shokonsha]]'') | leadership = | website = | architecture_style = | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = }} {{ill|Miyazaki Gokoku Shrine|ja|宮崎縣護國神社}} is a [[Gokoku shrine]] located in [[Japan]], dedicated to over 40,000 natives of [[Miyazaki Prefecture]] who died in war. In comparison to other [[Gokoku shrines]] its building took longer because of a lack of well known [[military history]] of [[Miyazaki Prefecture]], since the prefecture was made during the abolition of the provincial system by combinging parts of many provinces without its own local identiy.https://www.mlit.go.jp/tagengo-db/common/001565649.pdf The shrine was only completed in 1955 after the end of the [[Occupation of Japan]]. There was a government coverup of the [[COVID-19]] infected governor going there.{{Cite web |title=Officials tried to hide shrine visits by infected Miyazaki chief {{!}} The Asahi Shimbun: Breaking News, Japan News and Analysis |url=https://www.asahi.com/ajw/articles/14808701 |access-date=2023-10-25 |website=The Asahi Shimbun |language=en}} It has its own [[Chinju no Mori]] with feral roosters living in it{{Cite web |title=The Miyazaki Shrine Forest - Part 3 - Miyazaki |url=https://en.japantravel.com/miyazaki/the-miyazaki-shrine-forest-part-3/5468 |access-date=2023-04-23 |website=JapanTravel |date=8 July 2013 |language=en}} Sand was dedicated to it{{Cite news |date=2023-08-15 |title=Iwo Jima sand returned 'home' to relatives of WWII Japanese soldiers via US researcher |language=en |work=Mainichi Daily News |url=https://mainichi.jp/english/articles/20230815/p2a/00m/0na/011000c |access-date=2023-10-25}} {{ill|Miyazaki Gokoku Shrine|ja|宮崎縣護國神社}} , alongside {{ill|Kumamoto Gokoku Shrine|ja|熊本県護国神社}} were completed after World War II, when the war ended and after the [[Ministry of home affairs|Ministry of Home Affairs]] was abolished, so they were not designated as [[Gokoku Shrines]] by the Minister of Home Affairs and are actually correctly designated as "equivalent to designated Gokoku-jinja Shrine". == See Also == * [[Controversies surrounding [[Shinto]]| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Miyazaki Gokoku Shrine | image = [[File:Haiden of Miyazaki Prefecture Gokoku Shrine.jpg|250px|Miyazaki Gokoku Shrine]]
Haiden (March 2017) | map_type = Japan Miyazaki | latd = 31 | latm = 56 | lats = 13.67 | latNS = N | longd = 131 | longm = 25 | longs = 18.35 | longEW = E | coordinates_display = inline,title | coordinates_region = JP | location = 2-4-3 Jingu, Miyazaki City, Miyazaki Prefecture | deity = Spirits of those who served the nation and public from Miyazaki Prefecture, totaling 41,874 (as of March 2016)"70th Anniversary of the End of the Greater East Asia War" Memorial Book, p. 8 | founded = March 10, 1955 | annual_festival = April 10 | website = [https://miyazaki-gokoku.jp/ Miyazaki Gokoku Shrine] }} [[File:Miyazakiken-Gokoku-jinja, Mitama festival 01.jpg|thumb|250px|Lanterns for the Mitama festival in the shrine grounds]] {{nihongo|'''Miyazaki Gokoku Shrine'''|宮崎縣護國神社|Miyazaki-ken Gokoku Jinja}} is a {{ill|Gokoku shrine|en|護国神社}} located in {{ill|Miyazaki, Miyazaki|en|宮崎市}}. The shrine enshrines the spirits of 41,820 individuals who served the nation and public from Miyazaki Prefecture since the {{nihongo|Boshin War|戊辰戦争|Boshin sensō}}, along with 54 public servants who died in the line of duty (as of March 2016). == Overview == Most of the Gokoku shrines across Japan were originally established as {{ill|Shokonsha|en|招魂社}} during the {{nihongo|Meiji Restoration|明治維新|Meiji Ishin}}, with rituals for memorial services conducted before World War II. However, due to the fragmented state of Miyazaki Prefecture during the Meiji RestorationAt the time, the Miyazaki area ({{nihongo|Hyuga Province|日向国|Hyūga no kuni}}) was divided into various territories such as the {{nihongo|Bakufu|天領|}}, {{nihongo|Nobeoka Domain|延岡藩|}}, {{nihongo|Takanabe Domain|高鍋藩|}}, {{nihongo|Sadowara Domain|佐土原藩|}}, {{nihongo|Obi Domain|飫肥藩|}}, and {{nihongo|Satsuma Domain|薩摩藩|}}., there was no single Shokonsha to enshrine the spirits of those who died for the nation across the entire prefecture. Instead, small portable shrines were used for annual memorial services. Before World War II, there were seven Shokonsha shrines in Miyazaki Prefecture"Sociologica 27", pp. 36-37. They were all renamed Gokoku shrines on April 1, 1939, following an Interior Ministry order"Renaming Shokonsha to Gokoku Shrines" (Showa 14 Interior Ministry Ordinance No. 12) "Official Gazette" No. 3656, March 15, 1939, p. 510 ({{NDLJP|2960149/2}}). However, none of them were designated by the Interior Minister as official Gokoku shrines and were instead considered unofficial Gokoku shrines"Number of Gokoku Shrines" "Outline of Interior Ministry Affairs Showa 16", Interior Ministry, February 20, 1942, pp. 375-376 ({{NDLJP|1450040/571}}). {| class="wikitable" ! Shrine name (as of March 1942) ! location (then) ! location (now) ! Notes |- | Hirose Gokoku Shrine | Kitanaka District, Hirose Village | Sadowara Town, Miyazaki City | Originally Naka Shimoshima Shokonsha, founded by Sadowara Domain Lord {{nihongo|Shimazu Tadaoki|島津忠寛|}} |- | Fukushima Gokoku Shrine | Minaminaka District, Fukushima Town | Nishikata, Kushima City | Originally Nishikata Shokonsha, located on the rear mound of Fukushima Kofun No. 4 (Kenjōzuka) |- | Miyakonojo Gokoku Shrine | Miyamaru Town, Miyakonojo City | Komatsubara Town, Miyakonojo City (abolished) | Originally Miyamaru Shokonsha, dissolved as a religious corporation on October 2, 1956"Eighty Years of Miyazaki Prefecture Governance, Vol. 1", Miyazaki Prefecture, March 31, 1967, p. 883 ({{NDLJP|3030426/510}}) |- | Takaoka Gokoku Shrine | Higashimorokata District, Takaoka Town | Takaoka Town, Miyazaki City | Originally Morokata Uchiyama Shokonsha |- | Oyamada Gokoku Shrine (Muka Gokoku Shrine) | Higashimorokata District, Muka Village | Takaoka Town, Miyazaki City | Originally Morokata Muka Shokonsha, founded at Muka Castle site |- | Takanabe Gokoku Shrine | Koyu District, Takanabe Town | Same | Originally Takanabe Shokonsha, founded at Takanabe Castle site by Takanabe Domain |- | Aya Gokoku Shrine | Higashimorokata District, Aya VillageAya Town was established as a town on October 1, 1932, but the source follows the original designation. | Aya Town, Higashimorokata District | Privately established Shokonsha |} With the outbreak of the {{nihongo|Greater East Asia War|大東亜戦争|Daitōa sensō}} on December 8, 1941, the number of war dead increased significantly, deepening the public’s respect and gratitude for the spirits of the fallen. Consequently, the governor of Miyazaki Prefecture, {{nihongo|Nagafune Katsumi|長船克己|}}, and 58 representatives from various sectors formed the Miyazaki Prefecture Gokoku Shrine Construction Association. Although there were calls to establish the shrine in different parts of the prefecture, it was ultimately decided to build it in the prefectural capital, Miyazaki City, which lacked a designated Gokoku Shrine. On April 23, 1943, Interior Minister {{nihongo|Ando Kisaburo|安藤紀三郎|}} approved the establishment of the Miyazaki Gokoku Shrine to enshrine 3,358 spirits, with the groundbreaking ceremony held on May 10, 1943. Construction continued despite laborious efforts and material shortages, but the project was halted on May 31, 1945, due to intensified air raids. Following Japan’s defeat, the shrine construction was forcibly abandoned under the orders of the GHQ’s Miyazaki Civil Affairs Chief, Navy Lieutenant Masman. After the Treaty of San Francisco in 1952, efforts to rebuild the shrine resumed. Governor {{nihongo|Tanaka Nagashige|田中長茂|}} led the reorganization of the Miyazaki Prefecture Gokoku Shrine Rebuilding Association, which secured public donations to fund the project. On June 13, 1954, it was decided to establish the shrine within the precincts of {{nihongo|Miyazaki Jingu|宮崎神宮|}}, and the shrine was officially created on March 10, 1955. Subsequently, it was registered as a religious corporation on December 24, 1955[https://www.m-shinsei.jp/m_shrine/宮崎県護國神社（みやざきけんごこくじんじゃ）/ Miyazaki Gokoku Shrine (宮崎県護國神社)] Miyazaki Shrine Introduction Site, Miyazaki Prefecture Shinto Youth Association, April 20, 2012. == Facilities == [[File:View in Miyazaki Prefecture Gokoku Shrine.jpg|thumb|250px|Memorial monument within Miyazaki Gokoku Shrine (March 2017)]] * Relics Hall * Memorial monument for the war dead from the {{ill|Bougainville Island|en|ブーゲンビル島}} campaign * Monument for war dead from the 1st Taiwan Infantry Regiment from Miyazaki Prefecture * Monument for the war dead from the {{ill|37th Division (Imperial Japanese Army)|en|第37師団 (日本軍)}} * Monument for the 3710th Unit * Memorial for the Five Kamikaze Heroes of Shikishima Unit, including {{nihongo|Yukio Seki|関行男|}}, {{nihongo|Iwao Nakano|中野磐雄|}}, {{nihongo|Nobuo Tani|谷暢夫|}}, {{nihongo|Hajime Nagamine|永峰肇|}}, and {{nihongo|Shigeo Oguro|大黒繁男|}} * Monument for the {{nihongo|72nd Infantry Regiment|歩兵第72連隊|}} * Statue of the Kamikaze Heroes File:Miyazakiken-Gokoku-jinja, Memorial Center 01.jpg|Relics Hall == Relation with Miyazaki Jingu == Initially built in the western forest of Miyazaki Jingu, the shrine was operated jointly with Miyazaki Jingu. However, due to the wishes of many citizens, including bereaved families, for independent management, dedicated staff were appointed in April 1972. == festivals == * Grand festival (April 10) ** Initially held on March 10 to commemorate the shrine’s founding and enshrinement, but moved to April 10 in 1978 to accommodate better attendance and the schedules of key officials[https://miyazakijingu.or.jp/publics/index/32/detail=1/b_id=128/r_id=340/ Memorial] Miyazaki Jingu Blog, April 11, 2018. * Mitama festival (August 14-15) * Daily Memorial Service (11:00 AM) == Nearby facilities == * {{ill|Miyazaki Jingū|ja|宮崎神宮}} == Access == * 10-minute walk from {{nihongo|Miyazaki Jingu Station|宮崎神宮駅|}} on the {{nihongo|Nippō Main Line|日豊本線|Nippō Honsen}} * 5-minute walk from the Miyazaki Jingu bus stop on the {{nihongo|Miyazaki Kōtsū|宮崎交通|}} bus route == References == {{reflist}} == Further Reading == * {{Cite book|和書|title=大東亜戦争終結七十年記念誌 |publisher=宮崎県神道青年会 |url=https://www.m-shinsei.jp/kanri/wp-content/uploads/2022/11/70nen.pdf |date=2016-08-15}} * {{Cite journal |和書|author=森幸雄 |title=護国神社を事例とする、都市のシンボル的施設に対するイメージの変遷 |url=https://hdl.handle.net/10911/2440 |journal=Sociologica |volume=27 |issue=第1,2合併号 |publisher=[[Soka University]] |date=2003rd year 3月 |pages=25-45 |naid=110006608846}} == Related topics == * {{nihongo|Kawanami Gokoku Shrine|川南護国神社|1=Kawaminami Gokoku Shrine|}} - A Gokoku shrine established in Kawanami Town, Koyu District, Miyazaki Prefecture after World War II == External links == {{Commonscat|Miyazakiken-Gokoku-jinja}} * [https://miyazaki-gokoku.jp/ Miyazaki Gokoku Shrine] - Official website * {{Facebook|miyazakigokoku|宮﨑県護国神社}} {{citation needed|Miyazaki Gokoku Shrine}} {{Gokoku Shrines}} \ {{⚠️🚨COPIED from EverybodyWiki ❗❕⚠️😡😤Please respect Licence CC-BY-SA ❗}} {{Source Wikipedia}} {{draft categories| }} {{citation needed|Miyazaki Gokoku Shrine}} {{draft categories| [[Category:11]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Miyazaki Prefecture]] [[Category:Miyazaki, Miyazaki]] [[Category:Gokoku shrines]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Miyazaki]] [[Category:History of Miyazaki]] [[Category:Pages from everybodywiki under right title]] [[Category:Completion tier 2]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Miyazaki Gokoku Shrine]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Miyazaki Gokoku Shrine]][[Category:qq]] }} [[sn:Miyazaki Gokoku Shrine]] [[en:draft:Miyazaki Gokoku Shrine]] [[ja:宮崎縣護國神社]] [[en:Miyazaki Gokoku Shrine]] {{translated page|ja|宮崎縣護國神社|version=102022255|comment=Imported full ja history}}

Haiden (March 2017) | map_type = Japan Miyazaki | latd = 31 | latm = 56 | lats = 13.67 | latNS = N | longd = 131 | longm = 25 | longs = 18.35 | longEW = E | coordinates_display = inline,title | coordinates_region = JP | location = 2-4-3 Jingu, Miyazaki City, Miyazaki Prefecture | deity = Spirits of those who served the nation and public from Miyazaki Prefecture, totaling 41,874 (as of March 2016)"70th Anniversary of the End of the Greater East Asia War" Memorial Book, p. 8 | founded = March 10, 1955 | annual_festival = April 10 | website = [https://miyazaki-gokoku.jp/ Miyazaki Gokoku Shrine] }} [[File:Miyazakiken-Gokoku-jinja, Mitama festival 01.jpg|thumb|250px|Lanterns for the Mitama festival in the shrine grounds]] {{nihongo|'''Miyazaki Gokoku Shrine'''|宮崎縣護國神社|Miyazaki-ken Gokoku Jinja}} is a {{ill|Gokoku shrine|en|護国神社|12=draft}} located in {{ill|Miyazaki, Miyazaki|en|宮崎市|12=draft}}. The shrine enshrines the spirits of 41,820 individuals who served the nation and public from Miyazaki Prefecture since the {{nihongo|Boshin War|戊辰戦争|Boshin sensō}}, along with 54 public servants who died in the line of duty (as of March 2016). == Overview == Most of the Gokoku shrines across Japan were originally established as {{ill|Shokonsha|en|招魂社|12=draft}} during the {{nihongo|Meiji Restoration|明治維新|Meiji Ishin}}, with rituals for memorial services conducted before World War II. However, due to the fragmented state of Miyazaki Prefecture during the Meiji RestorationAt the time, the Miyazaki area ({{nihongo|Hyuga Province|日向国|Hyūga no kuni}}) was divided into various territories such as the {{nihongo|Bakufu|天領|}}, {{nihongo|Nobeoka Domain|延岡藩|}}, {{nihongo|Takanabe Domain|高鍋藩|}}, {{nihongo|Sadowara Domain|佐土原藩|}}, {{nihongo|Obi Domain|飫肥藩|}}, and {{nihongo|Satsuma Domain|薩摩藩|}}., there was no single Shokonsha to enshrine the spirits of those who died for the nation across the entire prefecture. Instead, small portable shrines were used for annual memorial services. Before World War II, there were seven Shokonsha shrines in Miyazaki Prefecture"Sociologica 27", pp. 36-37. They were all renamed Gokoku shrines on April 1, 1939, following an Interior Ministry order"Renaming Shokonsha to Gokoku Shrines" (Showa 14 Interior Ministry Ordinance No. 12) "Official Gazette" No. 3656, March 15, 1939, p. 510 ({{NDLJP|2960149/2}}). However, none of them were designated by the Interior Minister as official Gokoku shrines and were instead considered unofficial Gokoku shrines"Number of Gokoku Shrines" "Outline of Interior Ministry Affairs Showa 16", Interior Ministry, February 20, 1942, pp. 375-376 ({{NDLJP|1450040/571}}). {| class="wikitable" ! Shrine name (as of March 1942) ! location (then) ! location (now) ! Notes |- | Hirose Gokoku Shrine | Kitanaka District, Hirose Village | Sadowara Town, Miyazaki City | Originally Naka Shimoshima Shokonsha, founded by Sadowara Domain Lord {{nihongo|Shimazu Tadaoki|島津忠寛|}} |- | Fukushima Gokoku Shrine | Minaminaka District, Fukushima Town | Nishikata, Kushima City | Originally Nishikata Shokonsha, located on the rear mound of Fukushima Kofun No. 4 (Kenjōzuka) |- | Miyakonojo Gokoku Shrine | Miyamaru Town, Miyakonojo City | Komatsubara Town, Miyakonojo City (abolished) | Originally Miyamaru Shokonsha, dissolved as a religious corporation on October 2, 1956"Eighty Years of Miyazaki Prefecture Governance, Vol. 1", Miyazaki Prefecture, March 31, 1967, p. 883 ({{NDLJP|3030426/510}}) |- | Takaoka Gokoku Shrine | Higashimorokata District, Takaoka Town | Takaoka Town, Miyazaki City | Originally Morokata Uchiyama Shokonsha |- | Oyamada Gokoku Shrine (Muka Gokoku Shrine) | Higashimorokata District, Muka Village | Takaoka Town, Miyazaki City | Originally Morokata Muka Shokonsha, founded at Muka Castle site |- | Takanabe Gokoku Shrine | Koyu District, Takanabe Town | Same | Originally Takanabe Shokonsha, founded at Takanabe Castle site by Takanabe Domain |- | Aya Gokoku Shrine | Higashimorokata District, Aya VillageAya Town was established as a town on October 1, 1932, but the source follows the original designation. | Aya Town, Higashimorokata District | Privately established Shokonsha |} With the outbreak of the {{nihongo|Greater East Asia War|大東亜戦争|Daitōa sensō}} on December 8, 1941, the number of war dead increased significantly, deepening the public’s respect and gratitude for the spirits of the fallen. Consequently, the governor of Miyazaki Prefecture, {{nihongo|Nagafune Katsumi|長船克己|}}, and 58 representatives from various sectors formed the Miyazaki Prefecture Gokoku Shrine Construction Association. Although there were calls to establish the shrine in different parts of the prefecture, it was ultimately decided to build it in the prefectural capital, Miyazaki City, which lacked a designated Gokoku Shrine. On April 23, 1943, Interior Minister {{nihongo|Ando Kisaburo|安藤紀三郎|}} approved the establishment of the Miyazaki Gokoku Shrine to enshrine 3,358 spirits, with the groundbreaking ceremony held on May 10, 1943. Construction continued despite laborious efforts and material shortages, but the project was halted on May 31, 1945, due to intensified air raids. Following Japan’s defeat, the shrine construction was forcibly abandoned under the orders of the GHQ’s Miyazaki Civil Affairs Chief, Navy Lieutenant Masman. After the Treaty of San Francisco in 1952, efforts to rebuild the shrine resumed. Governor {{nihongo|Tanaka Nagashige|田中長茂|}} led the reorganization of the Miyazaki Prefecture Gokoku Shrine Rebuilding Association, which secured public donations to fund the project. On June 13, 1954, it was decided to establish the shrine within the precincts of {{nihongo|Miyazaki Jingu|宮崎神宮|}}, and the shrine was officially created on March 10, 1955. Subsequently, it was registered as a religious corporation on December 24, 1955[https://www.m-shinsei.jp/m_shrine/宮崎県護國神社（みやざきけんごこくじんじゃ）/ Miyazaki Gokoku Shrine (宮崎県護國神社)] Miyazaki Shrine Introduction Site, Miyazaki Prefecture Shinto Youth Association, April 20, 2012. == Facilities == [[File:View in Miyazaki Prefecture Gokoku Shrine.jpg|thumb|250px|Memorial monument within Miyazaki Gokoku Shrine (March 2017)]] * Relics Hall * Memorial monument for the war dead from the {{ill|Bougainville Island|en|ブーゲンビル島|12=draft}} campaign * Monument for war dead from the 1st Taiwan Infantry Regiment from Miyazaki Prefecture * Monument for the war dead from the {{ill|37th Division (Imperial Japanese Army)|en|第37師団 (日本軍)|12=draft}} * Monument for the 3710th Unit * Memorial for the Five Kamikaze Heroes of Shikishima Unit, including {{nihongo|Yukio Seki|関行男|}}, {{nihongo|Iwao Nakano|中野磐雄|}}, {{nihongo|Nobuo Tani|谷暢夫|}}, {{nihongo|Hajime Nagamine|永峰肇|}}, and {{nihongo|Shigeo Oguro|大黒繁男|}} * Monument for the {{nihongo|72nd Infantry Regiment|歩兵第72連隊|}} * Statue of the Kamikaze Heroes File:Miyazakiken-Gokoku-jinja, Memorial Center 01.jpg|Relics Hall == Relation with Miyazaki Jingu == Initially built in the western forest of Miyazaki Jingu, the shrine was operated jointly with Miyazaki Jingu. However, due to the wishes of many citizens, including bereaved families, for independent management, dedicated staff were appointed in April 1972. == festivals == * Grand festival (April 10) ** Initially held on March 10 to commemorate the shrine’s founding and enshrinement, but moved to April 10 in 1978 to accommodate better attendance and the schedules of key officials[https://miyazakijingu.or.jp/publics/index/32/detail=1/b_id=128/r_id=340/ Memorial] Miyazaki Jingu Blog, April 11, 2018. * Mitama festival (August 14-15) * Daily Memorial Service (11:00 AM) == Nearby facilities == * {{ill|Miyazaki Jingū|ja|宮崎神宮|12=draft}} == Access == * 10-minute walk from {{nihongo|Miyazaki Jingu Station|宮崎神宮駅|}} on the {{nihongo|Nippō Main Line|日豊本線|Nippō Honsen}} * 5-minute walk from the Miyazaki Jingu bus stop on the {{nihongo|Miyazaki Kōtsū|宮崎交通|}} bus route == References == {{reflist}} == Further Reading == * {{Cite book|和書|title=大東亜戦争終結七十年記念誌 |publisher=宮崎県神道青年会 |url=https://www.m-shinsei.jp/kanri/wp-content/uploads/2022/11/70nen.pdf |date=2016-08-15}} * {{Cite journal |和書|author=森幸雄 |title=護国神社を事例とする、都市のシンボル的施設に対するイメージの変遷 |url=https://hdl.handle.net/10911/2440 |journal=Sociologica |volume=27 |issue=第1,2合併号 |publisher=[[Soka University]] |date=2003rd year 3月 |pages=25-45 |naid=110006608846}} == Related topics == * {{nihongo|Kawanami Gokoku Shrine|川南護国神社|1=Kawaminami Gokoku Shrine|}} -
