However, none of them were designated by the Interior Minister as official Gokoku shrines and were instead considered unofficial Gokoku shrines"Number of Gokoku Shrines" "Outline of Interior Ministry Affairs Showa 16", Interior Ministry, February 20, 1942, pp. 375-376 ({{NDLJP|1450040/571}}). {| class="wikitable" ! Shrine name (as of March 1942) ! location (then) ! location (now) ! Notes |- | Hirose Gokoku Shrine | Kitanaka District, Hirose Village | Sadowara Town, Miyazaki City | Originally Naka Shimoshima Shokonsha, founded by Sadowara Domain Lord {{nihongo|Shimazu Tadaoki|島津忠寛|}} |- | Fukushima Gokoku Shrine | Minaminaka District, Fukushima Town | Nishikata, Kushima City | Originally Nishikata Shokonsha, located on the rear mound of Fukushima Kofun No. 4 (Kenjōzuka) |- | Miyakonojo Gokoku Shrine | Miyamaru Town, Miyakonojo City | Komatsubara Town, Miyakonojo City (abolished) | Originally Miyamaru Shokonsha, dissolved as a religious corporation on October 2, 1956"Eighty Years of Miyazaki Prefecture Governance, Vol. 1", Miyazaki Prefecture, March 31, 1967, p. 883 ({{NDLJP|3030426/510}}) |- | Takaoka Gokoku Shrine | Higashimorokata District, Takaoka Town | Takaoka Town, Miyazaki City | Originally Morokata Uchiyama Shokonsha |- | Oyamada Gokoku Shrine (Muka Gokoku Shrine) | Higashimorokata District, Muka Village | Takaoka Town, Miyazaki City | Originally Morokata Muka Shokonsha, founded at Muka Castle site |- | Takanabe Gokoku Shrine | Koyu District, Takanabe Town | Same | Originally Takanabe Shokonsha, founded at Takanabe Castle site by Takanabe Domain |- | Aya Gokoku Shrine | Higashimorokata District, Aya VillageAya Town was established as a town on October 1, 1932, but the source follows the original designation. | Aya Town, Higashimorokata District | Privately established Shokonsha |} With the outbreak of the {{nihongo|Greater East Asia War|大東亜戦争|Daitōa sensō}} on December 8, 1941, the number of war dead increased significantly, deepening the public’s respect and gratitude for the spirits of the fallen. Consequently, the governor of Miyazaki Prefecture, {{nihongo|Nagafune Katsumi|長船克己|}}, and 58 representatives from various sectors formed the Miyazaki Prefecture Gokoku Shrine Construction Association. Although there were calls to establish the shrine in different parts of the prefecture, it was ultimately decided to build it in the prefectural capital, Miyazaki City, which lacked a designated Gokoku Shrine. On April 23, 1943, Interior Minister {{nihongo|Ando Kisaburo|安藤紀三郎|}} approved the establishment of the Miyazaki Gokoku Shrine to enshrine 3,358 spirits, with the groundbreaking ceremony held on May 10, 1943. Construction continued despite laborious efforts and material shortages, but the project was halted on May 31, 1945, due to intensified air raids. Following Japan’s defeat, the shrine construction was forcibly abandoned under the orders of the GHQ’s Miyazaki Civil Affairs Chief, Navy Lieutenant Masman. After the Treaty of San Francisco in 1952, efforts to rebuild the shrine resumed. Governor {{nihongo|Tanaka Nagashige|田中長茂|}} led the reorganization of the Miyazaki Prefecture Gokoku Shrine Rebuilding Association, which secured public donations to fund the project. On June 13, 1954, it was decided to establish the shrine within the precincts of {{nihongo|Miyazaki Jingu|宮崎神宮|}}, and the shrine was officially created on March 10, 1955. Subsequently, it was registered as a religious corporation on December 24, 1955[https://www.m-shinsei.jp/m_shrine/宮崎県護國神社（みやざきけんごこくじんじゃ）/ Miyazaki Gokoku Shrine (宮崎県護國神社)] Miyazaki Shrine Introduction Site, Miyazaki Prefecture Shinto Youth Association, April 20, 2012. == Facilities == [[File:View in Miyazaki Prefecture Gokoku Shrine.jpg|thumb|250px|Memorial monument within Miyazaki Gokoku Shrine (March 2017)]] * Relics Hall * Memorial monument for the war dead from the {{ill|Bougainville Island|en|ブーゲンビル島|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Bougainville Island|qq=}} campaign * Monument for war dead from the 1st Taiwan Infantry Regiment from Miyazaki Prefecture * Monument for the war dead from the {{ill|37th Division (Imperial Japanese Army)|en|第37師団 (日本軍)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/37th Division (Imperial Japanese Army)|qq=}} * Monument for the 3710th Unit * Memorial for the Five Kamikaze Heroes of Shikishima Unit, including {{nihongo|Yukio Seki|関行男|}}, {{nihongo|Iwao Nakano|中野磐雄|}}, {{nihongo|Nobuo Tani|谷暢夫|}}, {{nihongo|Hajime Nagamine|永峰肇|}}, and {{nihongo|Shigeo Oguro|大黒繁男|}} * Monument for the {{nihongo|72nd Infantry Regiment|歩兵第72連隊|}} * Statue of the Kamikaze Heroes File:Miyazakiken-Gokoku-jinja, Memorial Center 01.jpg|Relics Hall == Relation with Miyazaki Jingu == Initially built in the western forest of Miyazaki Jingu, the shrine was operated jointly with Miyazaki Jingu. However, due to the wishes of many citizens, including bereaved families, for independent management, dedicated staff were appointed in April 1972. == festivals == * Grand festival (April 10) ** Initially held on March 10 to commemorate the shrine’s founding and enshrinement, but moved to April 10 in 1978 to accommodate better attendance and the schedules of key officials[https://miyazakijingu.or.jp/publics/index/32/detail=1/b_id=128/r_id=340/ Memorial] Miyazaki Jingu Blog, April 11, 2018. * Mitama festival (August 14-15) * Daily Memorial Service (11:00 AM) == Nearby facilities == * {{ill|Miyazaki Jingū|en|Miyazaki Jingū|WD=Q704686|lt=Miyazaki Jingū|1=Miyazaki Jingū}} == Access == * 10-minute walk from {{nihongo|Miyazaki Jingu Station|宮崎神宮駅|}} on the {{nihongo|Nippō Main Line|日豊本線|Nippō Honsen}} * 5-minute walk from the Miyazaki Jingu bus stop on the {{nihongo|Miyazaki Kōtsū|宮崎交通|}} bus route == References == {{reflist}} == Further Reading == * {{Cite book|和書|title=大東亜戦争終結七十年記念誌 |publisher=宮崎県神道青年会 |url=https://www.m-shinsei.jp/kanri/wp-content/uploads/2022/11/70nen.pdf |date=2016-08-15}} * {{Cite journal |和書|author=森幸雄 |title=護国神社を事例とする、都市のシンボル的施設に対するイメージの変遷 |url=https://hdl.handle.net/10911/2440 |journal=Sociologica |volume=27 |issue=第1,2合併号 |publisher=[[Soka University]] |date=2003rd year 3月 |pages=25-45 |naid=110006608846}} == See Also == * {{nihongo|Kawanami Gokoku Shrine|川南護国神社|1=Kawaminami Gokoku Shrine|}} - A Gokoku shrine established in Kawanami Town, Koyu District, Miyazaki Prefecture after World War II == External links == {{Commonscat|Miyazakiken-Gokoku-jinja}} * [https://miyazaki-gokoku.jp/ Miyazaki Gokoku Shrine] - Official website * {{Facebook|miyazakigokoku|宮﨑県護国神社}} {{Gokoku Shrines}} \ {{⚠️🚨COPIED from EverybodyWiki ❗❕⚠️😡😤Please respect Licence CC-BY-SA ❗}} {{translated page|ja|宮崎縣護國神社|version=102022255|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{everybodywiki link|Miyazaki Gokoku Shrine}} [[ja:宮崎縣護國神社]] {{Commons category|Miyazakiken-Gokoku-jinja}} {{wikidata link|Q120727713}} [[Category:Everybody linked]] [[Category:everybodywiki import successful]] [[Category:Gokoku shrines]] [[Category:Miyazaki, Miyazaki]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Miyazaki Prefecture]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:pages]]

[[co:commons:Category:Miyazakiken-Gokoku-jinja]][[ja:宮崎縣護國神社]] [[co:commons:Category:Miyazakiken-Gokoku-jinja]][[ja:宮崎縣護國神社]] [[Category:sn_crud|commons:Category:Miyazakiken-Gokoku-jinja]][[ja:宮崎縣護國神社]] {{Draft topics|east-asia}} {{AfC topic|geo}} {{Infobox religious building|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | image = File:Miyazakiken-Gokoku-jinja, Mitama Festival 01.jpg | map_type = Japan | coordinates ={{Coord|31.937130555555555|N|131.42176388888888|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | map_size = | map_alt = | map_relief = | map_caption = | location = | festival = | type = ''{{ill|Gokoku shrines|en|Gokoku shrines|WD=Q1534477|lt=Gokoku shrine|1=Gokoku Shrines}}''
(Formerly ''{{ill|Gokoku shrines|en|Gokoku shrines|WD=Q1534477|lt=Shokonsha|1=Gokoku Shrines}}'') | leadership = | website = | architecture_style = | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = | prefecture= {{ill|Miyazaki|WD=Q204308|1=Miyazaki (city)}} | deity= {{ill|eirei|WD=Q11617005|1=eirei}} }} {{ill|Miyazaki Gokoku Shrine|ja|宮崎縣護國神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Miyazaki Gokoku Shrine|qq=}} is a {{ill|Gokoku shrines|en|Gokoku shrines|WD=Q1534477|lt=Gokoku shrine|1=Gokoku Shrines}} located in {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan|1=Japan}}, dedicated to over 40,000 natives of {{ill|Miyazaki Prefecture|en|Miyazaki Prefecture|WD=Q130300|lt=Miyazaki Prefecture|1=Miyazaki Prefecture}} who died in war. In comparison to other {{ill|Gokoku shrines|en|Gokoku shrines|WD=Q1534477|lt=Gokoku shrines|1=Gokoku Shrines}} its building took longer because of a lack of well known {{ill|Military history|en|Military history|WD=Q192781|lt=Military history|1=Military history}} of {{ill|Miyazaki Prefecture|en|Miyazaki Prefecture|WD=Q130300|lt=Miyazaki Prefecture|1=Miyazaki Prefecture}}, since the prefecture was made during the abolition of the provincial system by combinging parts of many provinces without its own local identiy.https://www.mlit.go.jp/tagengo-db/common/001565649.pdf The shrine was only completed in 1955 after the end of the {{ill|Occupation of Japan|en|Occupation of Japan|WD=Q696251|lt=Occupation of Japan|1=Occupation of Japan}}. There was a government coverup of the {{ill|COVID-19|en|COVID-19|WD=Q84263196|lt=COVID-19|1=COVID-19}} infected governor going there.{{Cite web |title=Officials tried to hide shrine visits by infected Miyazaki chief {{!}} The Asahi Shimbun: Breaking News, Japan News and Analysis |url=https://www.asahi.com/ajw/articles/14808701 |access-date=2023-10-25 |website=The Asahi Shimbun |language=en}} It has its own [[Chinju no Mori]] with feral roosters living in it{{Cite web |title=The Miyazaki Shrine Forest - Part 3 - Miyazaki |url=https://en.japantravel.com/miyazaki/the-miyazaki-shrine-forest-part-3/5468 |access-date=2023-04-23 |website=JapanTravel |date=8 July 2013 |language=en}} Sand was dedicated to it{{Cite news |date=2023-08-15 |title=Iwo Jima sand returned 'home' to relatives of WWII Japanese soldiers via US researcher |language=en |work=Mainichi Daily News |url=https://mainichi.jp/english/articles/20230815/p2a/00m/0na/011000c |access-date=2023-10-25}} {{ill|Miyazaki Gokoku Shrine|ja|宮崎縣護國神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Miyazaki Gokoku Shrine|qq=}} , alongside {{ill|Kumamoto Gokoku Shrine|ja|熊本県護国神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumamoto Gokoku Shrine|qq=}} were completed after World War II, when the war ended and after the [[:en:Ministry of home affairs|Ministry of Home Affairs]] was abolished, so they were not designated as {{ill|Gokoku shrines|en|Gokoku shrines|WD=Q1534477|lt=Gokoku shrines|1=Gokoku Shrines}} by the Minister of Home Affairs and are actually correctly designated as "equivalent to designated Gokoku-jinja Shrine". == See Also == * [[Controversies surrounding Yasukuni Shrine]] * {{ill|Martyrs' shrines (China)|en|Martyrs' Wu temple|WD=Q7231588|lt=Wen Wu temple|1=Wen Wu temple}} * {{ill|National Revolutionary Martyrs' Shrine|en|National Revolutionary Martyrs' Shrine|WD=Q5956488|lt=National Revolutionary Martyrs' Shrine|1=National Revolutionary Martyrs' Shrine}} * {{ill|Eternal Spring Shrine|en|Eternal Spring Shrine|WD=Q4501089|lt=Eternal Spring Shrine|1=Eternal Spring Shrine}} * {{ill|Chinese Cultural Unknown Soldier|WD=Q1541043|lt=Tomb of the Unknown Soldier|1=Tomb of the Unknown Soldier}} * {{ill|Arlington National Cemetery|en|Arlington National Cemetery|WD=Q216344|lt=Arlington National Cemetery|1=Arlington National Cemetery}} * {{ill|Valhalla|en|Valhalla|WD=Q177860|lt=Valhalla|1=Valhalla}} (home to the souls of fallen warriors in Scandinavian mythology) * {{ill|Walhalla (memorial)|en|Walhalla (memorial)|WD=Q321240|lt=Walhalla Shrine|1=Walhalla (memorial)}} (a hall of fame in Germany honoring "commendable and honorable Germans") * {{ill|Eternal Spring Shrine|en|Eternal Spring Shrine|WD=Q4501089|lt=Eternal Spring Shrine|1=Eternal Spring Shrine}} * {{ill|The common end of myriad good deeds|en|The common end of myriad good deeds|WD=Q9609095|lt=The name = Miyazaki Gokoku Shrine | image = [[File:Haiden of Miyazaki Prefecture Gokoku Shrine.jpg|250px|Miyazaki Gokoku Shrine]]
Haiden (March 2017) | map_type = Japan Miyazaki | latd = 31 | latm = 56 | lats = 13.67 | latNS = N | longd = 131 | longm = 25 | longs = 18.35 | longEW = E | coordinates_display = inline,title | coordinates_region = JP | location = 2-4-3 Jingu, Miyazaki City, Miyazaki Prefecture | deity = Spirits of those who served the nation and public from Miyazaki Prefecture, totaling 41,874 (as of March 2016)"70th Anniversary of the End of the Greater East Asia War" Memorial Book, p. 8 | founded = March 10, 1955 | annual_festival = April 10 | website = [https://miyazaki-gokoku.jp/ Miyazaki Gokoku Shrine] }} [[File:Miyazakiken-Gokoku-jinja, Mitama festival 01.jpg|thumb|250px|Lanterns for the Mitama festival in the shrine grounds]] {{nihongo|'''Miyazaki Gokoku Shrine'''|宮崎縣護國神社|Miyazaki-ken Gokoku Jinja}} is a {{ill|Gokoku Shrines|lt=Gokoku Shrines|lt=Gokoku shrine|en|護国神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gokoku shrine|qq=}} located in {{ill|Miyazaki, Miyazaki|en|宮崎市|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Miyazaki, Miyazaki|qq=}}. The shrine enshrines the spirits of 41,820 individuals who served the nation and public from Miyazaki Prefecture since the {{nihongo|Boshin War|戊辰戦争|Boshin sensō}}, along with 54 public servants who died in the line of duty (as of March 2016). == Overview == Most of the Gokoku shrines across Japan were originally established as {{ill|Gokoku Shrines|lt=Gokoku Shrines|lt=Shokonsha|en|招魂社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shokonsha|qq=}} during the {{nihongo|Meiji Restoration|明治維新|Meiji Ishin}}, with rituals for memorial services conducted before World War II. However, due to the fragmented state of Miyazaki Prefecture during the Meiji RestorationAt the time, the Miyazaki area ({{nihongo|Hyūga Province|日向国|Hyūga no kuni}}) was divided into various territories such as the {{nihongo|Bakufu|天領|}}, {{nihongo|Nobeoka Domain|延岡藩|}}, {{nihongo|Takanabe Domain|高鍋藩|}}, {{nihongo|Sadowara Domain|佐土原藩|}}, {{nihongo|Obi Domain|飫肥藩|}}, and {{nihongo|Satsuma Domain|薩摩藩|}}., there was no single Shokonsha to enshrine the spirits of those who died for the nation across the entire prefecture. Instead, small portable shrines were used for annual memorial services. Before World War II, there were seven Shokonsha shrines in Miyazaki Prefecture"Sociologica 27", pp. 36-37. They were all renamed Gokoku shrines on April 1, 1939, following an Interior Ministry order"Renaming Shokonsha to Gokoku Shrines" (Showa 14 Interior Ministry Ordinance No. 12) "Official Gazette" No. 3656, March 15, 1939, p. 510 ({{NDLJP|2960149/2}}). However, none of them were designated by the Interior Minister as official Gokoku shrines and were instead considered unofficial Gokoku shrines"Number of Gokoku Shrines" "Outline of Interior Ministry Affairs Showa 16", Interior Ministry, February 20, 1942, pp. 375-376 ({{NDLJP|1450040/571}}). {| class="wikitable" ! Shrine name (as of March 1942) ! location (then) ! location (now) ! Notes |- | Hirose Gokoku Shrine | Kitanaka District, Hirose Village | Sadowara Town, Miyazaki City | Originally Naka Shimoshima Shokonsha, founded by Sadowara Domain Lord {{nihongo|Shimazu Tadaoki|島津忠寛|}} |- | Fukushima Gokoku Shrine | Minaminaka District, Fukushima Town | Nishikata, Kushima City | Originally Nishikata Shokonsha, located on the rear mound of Fukushima Kofun No. 4 (Kenjōzuka) |- | Miyakonojo Gokoku Shrine | Miyamaru Town, Miyakonojo City | Komatsubara Town, Miyakonojo City (abolished) | Originally Miyamaru Shokonsha, dissolved as a religious corporation on October 2, 1956"Eighty Years of Miyazaki Prefecture Governance, Vol. 1", Miyazaki Prefecture, March 31, 1967, p. 883 ({{NDLJP|3030426/510}}) |- | Takaoka Gokoku Shrine | Higashimorokata District, Takaoka Town | Takaoka Town, Miyazaki City | Originally Morokata Uchiyama Shokonsha |- | Oyamada Gokoku Shrine (Muka Gokoku Shrine) | Higashimorokata District, Muka Village | Takaoka Town, Miyazaki City | Originally Morokata Muka Shokonsha, founded at Muka Castle site |- | Takanabe Gokoku Shrine | Koyu District, Takanabe Town | Same | Originally Takanabe Shokonsha, founded at Takanabe Castle site by Takanabe Domain |- | Aya Gokoku Shrine | Higashimorokata District, Aya VillageAya Town was established as a town on October 1, 1932, but the source follows the original designation. | Aya Town, Higashimorokata District | Privately established Shokonsha |} With the outbreak of the {{nihongo|Greater East Asia War|大東亜戦争|Daitōa sensō}} on December 8, 1941, the number of war dead increased significantly, deepening the public’s respect and gratitude for the spirits of the fallen. Consequently, the governor of Miyazaki Prefecture, {{nihongo|Nagafune Katsumi|長船克己|}}, and 58 representatives from various sectors formed the Miyazaki Prefecture Gokoku Shrine Construction Association. Although there were calls to establish the shrine in different parts of the prefecture, it was ultimately decided to build it in the prefectural capital, Miyazaki City, which lacked a designated Gokoku Shrine. On April 23, 1943, Interior Minister {{nihongo|Ando Kisaburo|安藤紀三郎|}} approved the establishment of the Miyazaki Gokoku Shrine to enshrine 3,358 spirits, with the groundbreaking ceremony held on May 10, 1943. Construction continued despite laborious efforts and material shortages, but the project was halted on May 31, 1945, due to intensified air raids. Following Japan’s defeat, the shrine construction was forcibly abandoned under the orders of the GHQ’s Miyazaki Civil Affairs Chief, Navy Lieutenant Masman. After the Treaty of San Francisco in 1952, efforts to rebuild the shrine resumed. Governor {{nihongo|Tanaka Nagashige|田中長茂|}} led the reorganization of the Miyazaki Prefecture Gokoku Shrine Rebuilding Association, which secured public donations to fund the project. On June 13, 1954, it was decided to establish the shrine within the precincts of {{nihongo|Miyazaki Jingu|宮崎神宮|}}, and the shrine was officially created on March 10, 1955. Subsequently, it was registered as a religious corporation on December 24, 1955[https://www.m-shinsei.jp/m_shrine/宮崎県護國神社（みやざきけんごこくじんじゃ）/ Miyazaki Gokoku Shrine (宮崎県護國神社)] Miyazaki Shrine Introduction Site, Miyazaki Prefecture Shinto Youth Association, April 20, 2012. == Facilities == [[File:View in Miyazaki Prefecture Gokoku Shrine.jpg|thumb|250px|Memorial monument within Miyazaki Gokoku Shrine (March 2017)]] * Relics Hall * Memorial monument for the war dead from the {{ill|Bougainville Island|en|ブーゲンビル島|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Bougainville Island|qq=}} campaign * Monument for war dead from the 1st Taiwan Infantry Regiment from Miyazaki Prefecture * Monument for the war dead from the {{ill|37th Division (Imperial Japanese Army)|en|第37師団 (日本軍)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/37th Division (Imperial Japanese Army)|qq=}} * Monument for the 3710th Unit * Memorial for the Five Kamikaze Heroes of Shikishima Unit, including {{nihongo|Yukio Seki|関行男|}}, {{nihongo|Iwao Nakano|中野磐雄|}}, {{nihongo|Nobuo Tani|谷暢夫|}}, {{nihongo|Hajime Nagamine|永峰肇|}}, and {{nihongo|Shigeo Oguro|大黒繁男|}} * Monument for the {{nihongo|72nd Infantry Regiment|歩兵第72連隊|}} * Statue of the Kamikaze Heroes File:Miyazakiken-Gokoku-jinja, Memorial Center 01.jpg|Relics Hall == Relation with Miyazaki Jingu == Initially built in the western forest of Miyazaki Jingu, the shrine was operated jointly with Miyazaki Jingu. However, due to the wishes of many citizens, including bereaved families, for independent management, dedicated staff were appointed in April 1972. == festivals == * Grand festival (April 10) ** Initially held on March 10 to commemorate the shrine’s founding and enshrinement, but moved to April 10 in 1978 to accommodate better attendance and the schedules of key officials[https://miyazakijingu.or.jp/publics/index/32/detail=1/b_id=128/r_id=340/ Memorial] Miyazaki Jingu Blog, April 11, 2018. * Mitama festival (August 14-15) * Daily Memorial Service (11:00 AM) == Nearby facilities == * {{ill|Miyazaki Jingū|en|Miyazaki Jingū|WD=Q704686|lt=Miyazaki Jingū|1=Miyazaki Jingū}} == Access == * 10-minute walk from {{nihongo|Miyazaki Jingu Station|宮崎神宮駅|}} on the {{nihongo|Nippō Main Line|日豊本線|Nippō Honsen}} * 5-minute walk from the Miyazaki Jingu bus stop on the {{nihongo|Miyazaki Kōtsū|宮崎交通|}} bus route == References == {{reflist}} == Further Reading == * {{Cite book|和書|title=大東亜戦争終結七十年記念誌 |publisher=宮崎県神道青年会 |url=https://www.m-shinsei.jp/kanri/wp-content/uploads/2022/11/70nen.pdf |date=2016-08-15}} * {{Cite journal |和書|author=森幸雄 |title=護国神社を事例とする、都市のシンボル的施設に対するイメージの変遷 |url=https://hdl.handle.net/10911/2440 |journal=Sociologica |volume=27 |issue=第1,2合併号 |publisher=[[Soka University]] |date=2003rd year 3月 |pages=25-45 |naid=110006608846}} == See Also == * {{nihongo|Kawanami Gokoku Shrine|川南護国神社|1=Kawaminami Gokoku Shrine|}} - A Gokoku shrine established in Kawanami Town, Koyu District, Miyazaki Prefecture after World War II == External links == {{Commonscat|Miyazakiken-Gokoku-jinja}} * [https://miyazaki-gokoku.jp/ Miyazaki Gokoku Shrine] - [[co:commons:Category:Miyazakiken-Gokoku-jinja]][[ja:宮崎縣護國神社]] [[co:commons:Category:Miyazakiken-Gokoku-jinja]][[ja:宮崎縣護國神社]] [[Category:sn_crud|commons:Category:Miyazakiken-Gokoku-jinja]][[ja:宮崎縣護國神社]] {{Draft topics|east-asia}} {{AfC topic|geo}} {{Infobox religious building|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | image = File:Miyazakiken-Gokoku-jinja, Mitama Festival 01.jpg | map_type = Japan | coordinates ={{Coord|31.937130555555555|N|131.42176388888888|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | map_size = | map_alt = | map_relief = | map_caption = | location = | festival = | type = ''{{ill|Gokoku shrines|en|Gokoku shrines|WD=Q1534477|lt=Gokoku shrine|1=Gokoku Shrines}}''
(Formerly ''{{ill|Gokoku shrines|en|Gokoku shrines|WD=Q1534477|lt=Shokonsha|1=Gokoku Shrines}}'') | leadership = | website = | architecture_style = | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = | prefecture= {{ill|Miyazaki|WD=Q204308|1=Miyazaki (city)}} | deity= {{ill|eirei|WD=Q11617005|1=eirei}} }} {{ill|Miyazaki Gokoku Shrine|ja|宮崎縣護國神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Miyazaki Gokoku Shrine|qq=}} is a {{ill|Gokoku shrines|en|Gokoku shrines|WD=Q1534477|lt=Gokoku shrine|1=Gokoku Shrines}} located in {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan|1=Japan}}, dedicated to over 40,000 natives of {{ill|Miyazaki Prefecture|en|Miyazaki Prefecture|WD=Q130300|lt=Miyazaki Prefecture|1=Miyazaki Prefecture}} who died in war. In comparison to other {{ill|Gokoku shrines|en|Gokoku shrines|WD=Q1534477|lt=Gokoku shrines|1=Gokoku Shrines}} its building took longer because of a lack of well known {{ill|Military history|en|Military history|WD=Q192781|lt=Military history|1=Military history}} of {{ill|Miyazaki Prefecture|en|Miyazaki Prefecture|WD=Q130300|lt=Miyazaki Prefecture|1=Miyazaki Prefecture}}, since the prefecture was made during the abolition of the provincial system by combinging parts of many provinces without its own local identiy.https://www.mlit.go.jp/tagengo-db/common/001565649.pdf The shrine was only completed in 1955 after the end of the {{ill|Occupation of Japan|en|Occupation of Japan|WD=Q696251|lt=Occupation of Japan|1=Occupation of Japan}}. There was a government coverup of the {{ill|COVID-19|en|COVID-19|WD=Q84263196|lt=COVID-19|1=COVID-19}} infected governor going there.{{Cite web |title=Officials tried to hide shrine visits by infected Miyazaki chief {{!}} The Asahi Shimbun: Breaking News, Japan News and Analysis |url=https://www.asahi.com/ajw/articles/14808701 |access-date=2023-10-25 |website=The Asahi Shimbun |language=en}} It has its own [[Chinju no Mori]] with feral roosters living in it{{Cite web |title=The Miyazaki Shrine Forest - Part 3 - Miyazaki |url=https://en.japantravel.com/miyazaki/the-miyazaki-shrine-forest-part-3/5468 |access-date=2023-04-23 |website=JapanTravel |date=8 July 2013 |language=en}} Sand was dedicated to it{{Cite news |date=2023-08-15 |title=Iwo Jima sand returned 'home' to relatives of WWII Japanese soldiers via US researcher |language=en |work=Mainichi Daily News |url=https://mainichi.jp/english/articles/20230815/p2a/00m/0na/011000c |access-date=2023-10-25}} {{ill|Miyazaki Gokoku Shrine|ja|宮崎縣護國神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Miyazaki Gokoku Shrine|qq=}} , alongside {{ill|Kumamoto Gokoku Shrine|ja|熊本県護国神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumamoto Gokoku Shrine|qq=}} were completed after World War II, when the war ended and after the [[:en:Ministry of home affairs|Ministry of Home Affairs]] was abolished, so they were not designated as {{ill|Gokoku shrines|en|Gokoku shrines|WD=Q1534477|lt=Gokoku shrines|1=Gokoku Shrines}} by the Minister of Home Affairs and are actually correctly designated as "equivalent to designated Gokoku-jinja Shrine". == See Also == * [[Controversies surrounding Yasukuni Shrine]] * {{ill|Martyrs' shrines (China)|en|Martyrs' shrines (China)|WD=Q6800982|lt=Martyr's Shrines|1=Martyrs' shrines (China)}} * {{ill|Martial temple|en|Martial temple|WD=Q11124210|lt=Martial temple|1=Martial temple}} and {{ill|Wen Wu temple|en|Wen Wu temple|WD=Q7231588|lt=Wen Wu temple|1=Wen Wu temple}} * {{ill|National Revolutionary Martyrs' Shrine|en|National Revolutionary Martyrs' Shrine|WD=Q5956488|lt=National Revolutionary Martyrs' Shrine|1=National Revolutionary Martyrs' Shrine}} * {{ill|Eternal Spring Shrine|en|Eternal Spring Shrine|WD=Q4501089|lt=Eternal Spring Shrine|1=Eternal Spring Shrine}} * {{ill|Chinese Cultural Renaissance|en|Chinese Cultural Renaissance|WD=Q1073969|lt=Chinese Cultural Renaissance|1=Chinese Cultural Renaissance}} * [[Ancestral shrine]] * {{ill|Gallant Garden|en|Gallant Garden|WD=Q5518867|lt=Gallant Garden|1=Gallant Garden}} * {{ill|Gokoku shrines|en|Gokoku shrines|WD=Q1534477|lt=Gokoku shrines|1=Gokoku Shrines}} * {{ill|Tomb of the Unknown Soldier|en|Tomb of the Unknown Soldier|WD=Q1541043|lt=Tomb of the Unknown Soldier|1=Tomb of the Unknown Soldier}} * {{ill|Arlington National Cemetery|en|Arlington National Cemetery|WD=Q216344|lt=Arlington National Cemetery|1=Arlington National Cemetery}} * {{ill|Valhalla|en|Valhalla|WD=Q177860|lt=Valhalla|1=Valhalla}} (home to the souls of fallen warriors in Scandinavian mythology) * {{ill|Walhalla (memorial)|en|Walhalla (memorial)|WD=Q321240|lt=Walhalla Shrine|1=Walhalla (memorial)}} (a hall of fame in Germany honoring "commendable and honorable Germans") * {{ill|Eternal Spring Shrine|en|Eternal Spring Shrine|WD=Q4501089|lt=Eternal Spring Shrine|1=Eternal Spring Shrine}} * {{ill|The common end of myriad good deeds|en|The common end of myriad good deeds|WD=Q9609095|lt=The common end of myriad good deeds|1=The common end of myriad good deeds}} * {{ill|Greek hero cult|en|Greek hero cult|WD=Q1878906|lt=Greek hero cult|1=Greek hero cult}} {{Citation needed|date=2017-08-28}} {{Infobox religious building|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Miyazaki Gokoku Shrine | image = [[File:Haiden of Miyazaki Prefecture Gokoku Shrine.jpg|250px|Miyazaki Gokoku Shrine]]
Haiden (March 2017) | map_type = Japan Miyazaki | latd = 31 | latm = 56 | lats = 13.67 | latNS = N | longd = 131 | longm = 25 | longs = 18.35 | longEW = E | coordinates_display = inline,title | coordinates_region = JP | location = 2-4-3 Jingu, Miyazaki City, Miyazaki Prefecture | deity = Spirits of those who served the nation and public from Miyazaki Prefecture, totaling 41,874 (as of March 2016)"70th Anniversary of the End of the Greater East Asia War" Memorial Book, p. 8 | founded = March 10, 1955 | annual_festival = April 10 | website = [https://miyazaki-gokoku.jp/ Miyazaki Gokoku Shrine] }} [[File:Miyazakiken-Gokoku-jinja, Mitama festival 01.jpg|thumb|250px|Lanterns for the Mitama festival in the shrine grounds]] {{nihongo|'''Miyazaki Gokoku Shrine'''|宮崎縣護國神社|Miyazaki-ken Gokoku Jinja}} is a {{ill|Gokoku Shrines|lt=Gokoku Shrines|lt=Gokoku shrine|en|護国神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gokoku shrine|qq=}} located in {{ill|Miyazaki, Miyazaki|en|宮崎市|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Miyazaki, Miyazaki|qq=}}. The shrine enshrines the spirits of 41,820 individuals who served the nation and public from Miyazaki Prefecture since the {{nihongo|Boshin War|戊辰戦争|Boshin sensō}}, along with 54 public servants who died in the line of duty (as of March 2016). == Overview == Most of the Gokoku shrines across Japan were originally established as {{ill|Gokoku Shrines|lt=Gokoku Shrines|lt=Shokonsha|en|招魂社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shokonsha|qq=}} during the {{nihongo|Meiji Restoration|明治維新|Meiji Ishin}}, with rituals for memorial services conducted before World War II. However, due to the fragmented state of Miyazaki Prefecture during the Meiji RestorationAt the time, the Miyazaki area ({{nihongo|Hyūga Province|日向国|Hyūga no kuni}}) was divided into various territories such as the {{nihongo|Bakufu|天領|}}, {{nihongo|Nobeoka Domain|延岡藩|}}, {{nihongo|Takanabe Domain|高鍋藩|}}, {{nihongo|Sadowara Domain|佐土原藩|}}, {{nihongo|Obi Domain|飫肥藩|}}, and {{nihongo|Satsuma Domain|薩摩藩|}}., there was no single Shokonsha to enshrine the spirits of those who died for the nation across the entire prefecture. Instead, small portable shrines were used for annual memorial services. Before World War II, there were seven Shokonsha shrines in Miyazaki Prefecture"Sociologica 27", pp. 36-37. They were all renamed Gokoku shrines on April 1, 1939, following an Interior Ministry order"Renaming Shokonsha to Gokoku Shrines" (Showa 14 Interior Ministry Ordinance No. 12) "Official Gazette" No. 3656, March 15, 1939, p. 510 ({{NDLJP|2960149/2}}). However, none of them were designated by the Interior Minister as official Gokoku shrines and were instead considered unofficial Gokoku shrines"Number of Gokoku Shrines" "Outline of Interior Ministry Affairs Showa 16", Interior Ministry, February 20, 1942, pp. 375-376 ({{NDLJP|1450040/571}}). {| class="wikitable" ! Shrine name (as of March 1942) ! location (then) ! location (now) ! Notes |- | Hirose Gokoku Shrine | Kitanaka District, Hirose Village | Sadowara Town, Miyazaki City | Originally Naka Shimoshima Shokonsha, founded by Sadowara Domain Lord {{nihongo|Shimazu Tadaoki|島津忠寛|}} |- | Fukushima Gokoku Shrine | Minaminaka District, Fukushima Town | Nishikata, Kushima City | Originally Nishikata Shokonsha, located on the rear mound of Fukushima Kofun No. 4 (Kenjōzuka) |- | Miyakonojo Gokoku Shrine | Miyamaru Town, Miyakonojo City | Komatsubara Town, Miyakonojo City (abolished) | Originally Miyamaru Shokonsha, dissolved as a religious corporation on October 2, 1956"Eighty Years of Miyazaki Prefecture Governance, Vol. 1", Miyazaki Prefecture, March 31, 1967, p. 883 ({{NDLJP|3030426/510}}) |- | Takaoka Gokoku Shrine | Higashimorokata District, Takaoka Town | Takaoka Town, Miyazaki City | Originally Morokata Uchiyama Shokonsha |- | Oyamada Gokoku Shrine (Muka Gokoku Shrine) | Higashimorokata District, Muka Village | Takaoka Town, Miyazaki City | Originally Morokata Muka Shokonsha, founded at Muka Castle site |- | Takanabe Gokoku Shrine | Koyu District, Takanabe Town | Same | Originally Takanabe Shokonsha, founded at Takanabe Castle site by Takanabe Domain |- | Aya Gokoku Shrine | Higashimorokata District, Aya VillageAya Town was established as a town on October 1, 1932, but the source follows the original designation. | Aya Town, Higashimorokata District | Privately established Shokonsha |} With the outbreak of the {{nihongo|Greater East Asia War|大東亜戦争|Daitōa sensō}} on December 8, 1941, the number of war dead increased significantly, deepening the public’s respect and gratitude for the spirits of the fallen. Consequently, the governor of Miyazaki Prefecture, {{nihongo|Nagafune Katsumi|長船克己|}}, and 58 representatives from various sectors formed the Miyazaki Prefecture Gokoku Shrine Construction Association. Although there were calls to establish the shrine in different parts of the prefecture, it was ultimately decided to build it in the prefectural capital, Miyazaki City, which lacked a designated Gokoku Shrine. On April 23, 1943, Interior Minister {{nihongo|Ando Kisaburo|安藤紀三郎|}} approved the establishment of the Miyazaki Gokoku Shrine to enshrine 3,358 spirits, with the groundbreaking ceremony held on May 10, 1943. Construction continued despite laborious efforts and material shortages, but the project was halted on May 31, 1945, due to intensified air raids. Following Japan’s defeat, the shrine construction was forcibly abandoned under the orders of the GHQ’s Miyazaki Civil Affairs Chief, Navy Lieutenant Masman. After the Treaty of San Francisco in 1952, efforts to rebuild the shrine resumed. Governor {{nihongo|Tanaka Nagashige|田中長茂|}} led the reorganization of the Miyazaki Prefecture Gokoku Shrine Rebuilding Association, which secured public donations to fund the project. On June 13, 1954, it was decided to establish the shrine within the precincts of {{nihongo|Miyazaki Jingu|宮崎神宮|}}, and the shrine was officially created on March 10, 1955. Subsequently, it was registered as a religious corporation on December 24, 1955[https://www.m-shinsei.jp/m_shrine/宮崎県護國神社（みやざきけんごこくじんじゃ）/ Miyazaki Gokoku Shrine (宮崎県護國神社)] Miyazaki Shrine Introduction Site, Miyazaki Prefecture Shinto Youth Association, April 20, 2012. == Facilities == [[File:View in Miyazaki Prefecture Gokoku Shrine.jpg|thumb|250px|Memorial monument within Miyazaki Gokoku Shrine (March 2017)]] * Relics Hall * Memorial monument for the war dead from the {{ill|Bougainville Island|en|ブーゲンビル島|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Bougainville Island|qq=}} campaign * Monument for war dead from the 1st Taiwan Infantry Regiment from Miyazaki Prefecture * Monument for the war dead from the {{ill|37th Division (Imperial Japanese Army)|en|第37師団 (日本軍)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/37th Division (Imperial Japanese Army)|qq=}} * Monument for the 3710th Unit * Memorial for the Five Kamikaze Heroes of Shikishima Unit, including {{nihongo|Yukio Seki|関行男|}}, {{nihongo|Iwao Nakano|中野磐雄|}}, {{nihongo|Nobuo Tani|谷暢夫|}}, {{nihongo|Hajime Nagamine|永峰肇|}}, and {{nihongo|Shigeo Oguro|大黒繁男|}} * Monument for the {{nihongo|72nd Infantry Regiment|歩兵第72連隊|}} * Statue of the Kamikaze Heroes File:Miyazakiken-Gokoku-jinja, Memorial Center 01.jpg|Relics Hall == Relation with Miyazaki Jingu == Initially built in the western forest of Miyazaki Jingu, the shrine was operated jointly with Miyazaki Jingu. However, due to the wishes of many citizens, including bereaved families, for independent management, dedicated staff were appointed in April 1972. == festivals == * Grand festival (April 10) ** Initially held on March 10 to commemorate the shrine’s founding and enshrinement, but moved to April 10 in 1978 to accommodate better attendance and the schedules of key officials[https://miyazakijingu.or.jp/publics/index/32/detail=1/b_id=128/r_id=340/ Memorial] Miyazaki Jingu Blog, April 11, 2018. * Mitama festival (August 14-15) * Daily Memorial Service (11:00 AM) == Nearby facilities == * {{ill|Miyazaki Jingū|en|Miyazaki Jingū|WD=Q704686|lt=Miyazaki Jingū|1=Miyazaki Jingū}} == Access == * 10-minute walk from {{nihongo|Miyazaki Jingu Station|宮崎神宮駅|}} on the {{nihongo|Nippō Main Line|日豊本線|Nippō Honsen}} * 5-minute walk from the Miyazaki Jingu bus stop on the {{nihongo|Miyazaki Kōtsū|宮崎交通|}} bus route == References == {{reflist}} == Further Reading == * {{Cite book|和書|title=大東亜戦争終結七十年記念誌 |publisher=宮崎県神道青年会 |url=https://www.m-shinsei.jp/kanri/wp-content/uploads/2022/11/70nen.pdf |date=2016-08-15}} * {{Cite journal |和書|author=森幸雄 |title=護国神社を事例とする、都市のシンボル的施設に対するイメージの変遷 |url=https://hdl.handle.net/10911/2440 |journal=Sociologica |volume=27 |issue=第1,2合併号 |publisher=[[Soka University]] |date=2003rd year 3月 |pages=25-45 |naid=110006608846}} == See Also == * {{nihongo|Kawanami Gokoku Shrine|川南護国神社|1=Kawaminami Gokoku Shrine|}} - A Gokoku shrine established in Kawanami Town, Koyu District, Miyazaki Prefecture after World War II == External links == {{Commonscat|Miyazakiken-Gokoku-jinja}} * [https://miyazaki-gokoku.jp/ Miyazaki Gokoku Shrine] - Official website * {{Facebook|miyazakigokoku|宮﨑県護国神社}} {{Gokoku Shrines}} \ {{⚠️🚨COPIED from EverybodyWiki ❗❕⚠️😡😤Please respect Licence CC-BY-SA ❗}} {{translated page|ja|宮崎縣護國神社|version=102022255|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{everybodywiki link|Miyazaki Gokoku Shrine}} [[ja:宮崎縣護國神社]] {{Commons category|Miyazakiken-Gokoku-jinja}} {{wikidata link|Q120727713}} [[Category:everybodywiki import successful]] [[Category:Gokoku shrines]] [[Category:Miyazaki, Miyazaki]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Miyazaki Prefecture]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]]

Haiden (March 2017) | map_type = Japan Miyazaki | latd = 31 | latm = 56 | lats = 13.67 | latNS = N | longd = 131 | longm = 25 | longs = 18.35 | longEW = E | coordinates_display = inline,title | coordinates_region = JP | location = 2-4-3 Jingu, Miyazaki City, Miyazaki Prefecture | deity = Spirits of those who served the nation and public from Miyazaki Prefecture, totaling 41,874 (as of March 2016)"70th Anniversary of the End of the Greater East Asia War" Memorial Book, p. 8 | founded = March 10, 1955 | annual_festival = April 10 | website = [https://miyazaki-gokoku.jp/ Miyazaki Gokoku Shrine] }} [[File:Miyazakiken-Gokoku-jinja, Mitama festival 01.jpg|thumb|250px|Lanterns for the Mitama festival in the shrine grounds]] {{nihongo|'''Miyazaki Gokoku Shrine'''|宮崎縣護國神社|Miyazaki-ken Gokoku Jinja}} is a {{ill|Gokoku Shrines|lt=Gokoku Shrines|lt=Gokoku shrine|en|護国神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gokoku shrine|qq=}} located in {{ill|Miyazaki, Miyazaki|en|宮崎市|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Miyazaki, Miyazaki|qq=}}. The shrine enshrines the spirits of 41,820 individuals who served the nation and public from Miyazaki Prefecture since the {{nihongo|Boshin War|戊辰戦争|Boshin sensō}}, along with 54 public servants who died in the line of duty (as of March 2016). == Overview == Most of the Gokoku shrines across Japan were originally established as {{ill|Gokoku Shrines|lt=Gokoku Shrines|lt=Shokonsha|en|招魂社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shokonsha|qq=}} during the {{nihongo|Meiji Restoration|明治維新|Meiji Ishin}}, with rituals for memorial services conducted before World War II. However, due to the fragmented state of Miyazaki Prefecture during the Meiji RestorationAt the time, the Miyazaki area ({{nihongo|Hyūga Province|日向国|Hyūga no kuni}}) was divided into various territories such as the {{nihongo|Bakufu|天領|}}, {{nihongo|Nobeoka Domain|延岡藩|}}, {{nihongo|Takanabe Domain|高鍋藩|}}, {{nihongo|Sadowara Domain|佐土原藩|}}, {{nihongo|Obi Domain|飫肥藩|}}, and {{nihongo|Satsuma Domain|薩摩藩|}}., there was no single Shokonsha to enshrine the spirits of those who died for the nation across the entire prefecture. Instead, small portable shrines were used for annual memorial services. Before World War II, there were seven Shokonsha shrines in Miyazaki Prefecture"Sociologica 27", pp. 36-37. They were all renamed Gokoku shrines on April 1, 1939, following an Interior Ministry order"Renaming Shokonsha to Gokoku Shrines" (Showa 14 Interior Ministry Ordinance No. 12) "Official Gazette" No. 3656, March 15, 1939, p. 510 ({{NDLJP|2960149/2}}). However, none of them were designated by the Interior Minister as official Gokoku shrines and were instead considered unofficial Gokoku shrines"Number of Gokoku Shrines" "Outline of Interior Ministry Affairs Showa 16", Interior Ministry, February 20, 1942, pp. 375-376 ({{NDLJP|1450040/571}}). {| class="wikitable" ! Shrine name (as of March 1942) ! location (then) ! location (now) ! Notes |- | Hirose Gokoku Shrine | Kitanaka District, Hirose Village | Sadowara Town, Miyazaki City | Originally Naka Shimoshima Shokonsha, founded by Sadowara Domain Lord {{nihongo|Shimazu Tadaoki|島津忠寛|}} |- | Fukushima Gokoku Shrine | Minaminaka District, Fukushima Town | Nishikata, Kushima City | Originally Nishikata Shokonsha, located on the rear mound of Fukushima Kofun No. 4 (Kenjōzuka) |- | Miyakonojo Gokoku Shrine | Miyamaru Town, Miyakonojo City | Komatsubara Town, Miyakonojo City (abolished) | Originally Miyamaru Shokonsha, dissolved as a religious corporation on October 2, 1956"Eighty Years of Miyazaki Prefecture Governance, Vol. 1", Miyazaki Prefecture, March 31, 1967, p. 883 ({{NDLJP|3030426/510}}) |- | Takaoka Gokoku Shrine | Higashimorokata District, Takaoka Town | Takaoka Town, Miyazaki City | Originally Morokata Uchiyama Shokonsha |- | Oyamada Gokoku Shrine (Muka Gokoku Shrine) | Higashimorokata District, Muka Village | Takaoka Town, Miyazaki City | Originally Morokata Muka Shokonsha, founded at Muka Castle site |- | Takanabe Gokoku Shrine | Koyu District, Takanabe Town | Same | Originally Takanabe Shokonsha, founded at Takanabe Castle site by Takanabe Domain |- | Aya Gokoku Shrine | Higashimorokata District, Aya VillageAya Town was established as a town on October 1, 1932, but the source follows the original designation. | Aya Town, Higashimorokata District | Privately established Shokonsha |} With the outbreak of the {{nihongo|Greater East Asia War|大東亜戦争|Daitōa sensō}} on December 8, 1941, the number of war dead increased significantly, deepening the public’s respect and gratitude for the spirits of the fallen. Consequently, the governor of Miyazaki Prefecture, {{nihongo|Nagafune Katsumi|長船克己|}}, and 58 representatives from various sectors formed the Miyazaki Prefecture Gokoku Shrine Construction Association. Although there were calls to establish the shrine in different parts of the prefecture, it was ultimately decided to build it in the prefectural capital, Miyazaki City, which lacked a designated Gokoku Shrine. On April 23, 1943, Interior Minister {{nihongo|Ando Kisaburo|安藤紀三郎|}} approved the establishment of the Miyazaki Gokoku Shrine to enshrine 3,358 spirits, with the groundbreaking ceremony held on May 10, 1943. Construction continued despite laborious efforts and material shortages, but the project was halted on May 31, 1945, due to intensified air raids. Following Japan’s defeat, the shrine construction was forcibly abandoned under the orders of the GHQ’s Miyazaki Civil Affairs Chief, Navy Lieutenant Masman. After the Treaty of San Francisco in 1952, efforts to rebuild the shrine resumed. Governor {{nihongo|Tanaka Nagashige|田中長茂|}} led the reorganization of the Miyazaki Prefecture Gokoku Shrine Rebuilding Association, which secured public donations to fund the project. On June 13, 1954, it was decided to establish the shrine within the precincts of {{nihongo|Miyazaki Jingu|宮崎神宮|}}, and the shrine was officially created on March 10, 1955. Subsequently, it was registered as a religious corporation on December 24, 1955[https://www.m-shinsei.jp/m_shrine/宮崎県護國神社（みやざきけんごこくじんじゃ）/ Miyazaki Gokoku Shrine (宮崎県護國神社)] Miyazaki Shrine Introduction Site, Miyazaki Prefecture Shinto Youth Association, April 20, 2012. == Facilities == [[File:View in Miyazaki Prefecture Gokoku Shrine.jpg|thumb|250px|Memorial monument within Miyazaki Gokoku Shrine (March 2017)]] * Relics Hall * Memorial monument for the war dead from the {{ill|Bougainville Island|en|ブーゲンビル島|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Bougainville Island|qq=}} campaign * Monument for war dead from the 1st Taiwan Infantry Regiment from Miyazaki Prefecture * Monument for the war dead from the {{ill|37th Division (Imperial Japanese Army)|en|第37師団 (日本軍)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/37th Division (Imperial Japanese Army)|qq=}} * Monument for the 3710th Unit * Memorial for the Five Kamikaze Heroes of Shikishima Unit, including {{nihongo|Yukio Seki|関行男|}}, {{nihongo|Iwao Nakano|中野磐雄|}}, {{nihongo|Nobuo Tani|谷暢夫|}}, {{nihongo|Hajime Nagamine|永峰肇|}}, and {{nihongo|Shigeo Oguro|大黒繁男|}} * Monument for the {{nihongo|72nd Infantry Regiment|歩兵第72連隊|}} * Statue of the Kamikaze Heroes File:Miyazakiken-Gokoku-jinja, Memorial Center 01.jpg|Relics Hall == Relation with Miyazaki Jingu == Initially built in the western forest of Miyazaki Jingu, the shrine was operated jointly with Miyazaki Jingu. However, due to the wishes of many citizens, including bereaved families, for independent management, dedicated staff were appointed in April 1972. == festivals == * Grand festival (April 10) ** Initially held on March 10 to commemorate the shrine’s founding and enshrinement, but moved to April 10 in 1978 to accommodate better attendance and the schedules of key officials[https://miyazakijingu.or.jp/publics/index/32/detail=1/b_id=128/r_id=340/ Memorial] Miyazaki Jingu Blog, April 11, 2018. * Mitama festival (August 14-15) * Daily Memorial Service (11:00 AM) == Nearby facilities == * {{ill|Miyazaki Jingū|en|Miyazaki Jingū|WD=Q704686|lt=Miyazaki Jingū|1=Miyazaki Jingū}} == Access == * 10-minute walk from {{nihongo|Miyazaki Jingu Station|宮崎神宮駅|}} on the {{nihongo|Nippō Main Line|日豊本線|Nippō Honsen}} * 5-minute walk from the Miyazaki Jingu bus stop on the {{nihongo|Miyazaki Kōtsū|宮崎交通|}} bus route == References == {{reflist}} == Further Reading == * {{Cite book|和書|title=大東亜戦争終結七十年記念誌 |publisher=宮崎県神道青年会 |url=https://www.m-shinsei.jp/kanri/wp-content/uploads/2022/11/70nen.pdf |date=2016-08-15}} * {{Cite journal |和書|author=森幸雄 |title=護国神社を事例とする、都市のシンボル的施設に対するイメージの変遷 |url=https://hdl.handle.net/10911/2440 |journal=Sociologica |volume=27 |issue=第1,2合併号 |publisher=[[Soka University]] |date=2003rd year 3月 |pages=25-45 |naid=110006608846}} == See Also == * {{nihongo|Kawanami Gokoku Shrine|川南護国神社|1=Kawaminami Gokoku Shrine|}} - A Gokoku shrine established in Kawanami Town, Koyu District, Miyazaki Prefecture after World War II == External links == {{Commonscat|Miyazakiken-Gokoku-jinja}} * [https://miyazaki-gokoku.jp/ Miyazaki Gokoku Shrine] - In comparison to other {{ill|Gokoku shrines|en|Gokoku shrines|qid=Q1534477|lt=Gokoku shrines|1=Gokoku Shrines}} its building took longer because of a lack of well known {{ill|Military history|en|Military history|qid=Q192781|lt=Military history|1=Military history}} of {{ill|Miyazaki Prefecture|en|Miyazaki Prefecture|qid=Q130300|lt=Miyazaki Prefecture|1=Miyazaki Prefecture}}, since the prefecture was made during the abolition of the provincial system by combinging parts of many provinces without its own local identiy.https://www.mlit.go.jp/tagengo-db/common/001565649.pdf The shrine was only completed in 1955 after the end of the {{ill|Occupation of Japan|en|Occupation of Japan|qid=Q696251|lt=Occupation of Japan|1=Occupation of Japan}}. Consequently, the governor of Miyazaki Prefecture, {{nihongo|Nagafune Katsumi|長船克己|}}, and 58 representatives from various sectors formed the Miyazaki Prefecture Gokoku Shrine Construction Association. On April 23, 1943, Interior Minister {{nihongo|Ando Kisaburo|安藤紀三郎|}} approved the establishment of the Miyazaki Gokoku Shrine to enshrine 3,358 spirits, with the groundbreaking ceremony held on May 10, 1943. After the Treaty of San Francisco in 1952, efforts to rebuild the shrine resumed. On June 13, 1954, it was decided to establish the shrine within the precincts of {{nihongo|Miyazaki Jingu|宮崎神宮|}}, and the shrine was officially created on March 10, 1955. Subsequently, it was registered as a religious corporation on December 24, 1955[https://www.m-shinsei.jp/m_shrine/宮崎県護國神社（みやざきけんごこくじんじゃ）/ Miyazaki Gokoku Shrine (宮崎県護國神社)] Miyazaki Shrine Introduction Site, Miyazaki Prefecture Shinto Youth Association, April 20, 2012. == Facilities == [[File:View in Miyazaki Prefecture Gokoku Shrine.jpg|thumb|250px|Memorial monument within Miyazaki Gokoku Shrine (March 2017)]] * Relics Hall * Memorial monument for the war dead from the {{ill|Bougainville Island|en|ブーゲンビル島|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Bougainville Island|qq=}} campaign * Monument for war dead from the 1st Taiwan Infantry Regiment from Miyazaki Prefecture * Monument for the war dead from the {{ill|37th Division (Imperial Japanese Army)|en|第37師団 (日本軍)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/37th Division (Imperial Japanese Army)|qq=}} * Monument for the 3710th Unit * Memorial for the Five Kamikaze Heroes of Shikishima Unit, including {{nihongo|Yukio Seki|関行男|}}, {{nihongo|Iwao Nakano|中野磐雄|}}, {{nihongo|Nobuo Tani|谷暢夫|}}, {{nihongo|Hajime Nagamine|永峰肇|}}, and {{nihongo|Shigeo Oguro|大黒繁男|}} * Monument for the {{nihongo|72nd Infantry Regiment|歩兵第72連隊|}} * Statue of the Kamikaze Heroes File:Miyazakiken-Gokoku-jinja, Memorial Center 01.jpg|Relics Hall == Relation with Miyazaki Jingu == Initially built in the western forest of Miyazaki Jingu, the shrine was operated jointly with Miyazaki Jingu. However, due to the wishes of many citizens, including bereaved families, for independent management, dedicated staff were appointed in April 1972. == festivals == * Grand festival (April 10) ** Initially held on March 10 to commemorate the shrine’s founding and enshrinement, but moved to April 10 in 1978 to accommodate better attendance and the schedules of key officials[https://miyazakijingu.or.jp/publics/index/32/detail=1/b_id=128/r_id=340/ Memorial] Miyazaki Jingu Blog, April 11, 2018. * Mitama festival (August 14-15) * Daily Memorial Service (11:00 AM) == Nearby facilities == * {{ill|Miyazaki Jingū|en|Miyazaki Jingū|qid=Q704686|lt=Miyazaki Jingū|1=Miyazaki Jingū}} == Access == * 10-minute walk from {{nihongo|Miyazaki Jingu Station|宮崎神宮駅|}} on the {{nihongo|Nippō Main Line|日豊本線|Nippō Honsen}} * 5-minute walk from the Miyazaki Jingu bus stop on the {{nihongo|Miyazaki Kōtsū|宮崎交通|}} bus route == References == {{reflist}} == Further Reading == * {{Cite book|和書|title=大東亜戦争終結七十年記念誌 |publisher=宮崎県神道青年会 |url=https://www.m-shinsei.jp/kanri/wp-content/uploads/2022/11/70nen.pdf |date=2016-08-15}} * {{Cite journal |和書|author=森幸雄 |title=護国神社を事例とする、都市のシンボル的施設に対するイメージの変遷 |url=https://hdl.handle.net/10911/2440 |journal=Sociologica |volume=27 |issue=第1,2合併号 |publisher=[[Soka University]] |date=2003rd year 3月 |pages=25-45 |naid=110006608846}} == See Also == * {{nihongo|Kawanami Gokoku Shrine|川南護国神社|1=Kawaminami Gokoku Shrine|}} - A Gokoku shrine established in Kawanami Town, Koyu District, Miyazaki Prefecture after World War II == External links == {{Commonscat|Miyazakiken-Gokoku-jinja}} * [https://miyazaki-gokoku.jp/ Miyazaki Gokoku Shrine] - Official website * {{Facebook|miyazakigokoku|宮﨑県護国神社}} {{Gokoku Shrines}} \ {{⚠️🚨COPIED from EverybodyWiki ❗❕⚠️😡😤Please respect Licence CC-BY-SA ❗}} {{translated page|ja|宮崎縣護國神社|version=102022255|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{everybodywiki link|Miyazaki Gokoku Shrine}} [[ja:宮崎縣護國神社]] {{Commons category|Miyazakiken-Gokoku-jinja}} {{wikidata link|Q120727713}} [[Category:Gokoku shrines]] [[Category:Miyazaki, Miyazaki]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Miyazaki Prefecture]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]]

[[co:commons:Category:Miyazakiken-Gokoku-jinja]][[ja:宮崎縣護國神社]] [[co:commons:Category:Miyazakiken-Gokoku-jinja]][[ja:宮崎縣護國神社]] [[ja:宮崎縣護國神社]] {{Draft topics|east-asia}} {{AfC topic|geo}} {{Infobox religious building|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | image = File:Miyazakiken-Gokoku-jinja, Mitama Festival 01.jpg | map_type = Japan | coordinates ={{Coord|31.937130555555555|N|131.42176388888888|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | map_size = | map_alt = | map_relief = | map_caption = | location = | festival = | type = ''{{ill|Gokoku shrines|en|Gokoku shrines|qid=Q1534477|lt=Gokoku shrine|1=Gokoku Shrines}}''
(Formerly ''{{ill|Gokoku shrines|en|Gokoku shrines|qid=Q1534477|lt=Shokonsha|1=Gokoku Shrines}}'') | leadership = | website = | architecture_style = | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = | prefecture= {{ill|Miyazaki|qid=Q204308|1=Miyazaki (city)}} | deity= {{ill|eirei|qid=Q11617005|1=eirei}} }} {{ill|Miyazaki Gokoku Shrine|ja|宮崎縣護國神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Miyazaki Gokoku Shrine|qq=}} is a {{ill|Gokoku shrines|en|Gokoku shrines|qid=Q1534477|lt=Gokoku shrine|1=Gokoku Shrines}} located in {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|qid=Q17|lt=Japan|1=Japan}}, dedicated to over 40,000 natives of {{ill|Miyazaki Prefecture|en|Miyazaki Prefecture|qid=Q130300|lt=Miyazaki Prefecture|1=Miyazaki Prefecture}} who died in war. In comparison to other {{ill|Gokoku shrines|en|Gokoku shrines|qid=Q1534477|lt=Gokoku shrines|1=Gokoku Shrines}} its building took longer because of a lack of well known {{ill|Military history|en|Military history|qid=Q192781|lt=Military history|1=Military history}} of {{ill|Miyazaki Prefecture|en|Miyazaki Prefecture|qid=Q130300|lt=Miyazaki Prefecture|1=Miyazaki Prefecture}}, since the prefecture was made during the abolition of the provincial system by combinging parts of many provinces without its own local identiy.https://www.mlit.go.jp/tagengo-db/common/001565649.pdf The shrine was only completed in 1955 after the end of the {{ill|Occupation of Japan|en|Occupation of Japan|qid=Q696251|lt=Occupation of Japan|1=Occupation of Japan}}. There was a government coverup of the {{ill|COVID-19|en|COVID-19|qid=Q84263196|lt=COVID-19|1=COVID-19}} infected governor going there.{{Cite web |title=Officials tried to hide shrine visits by infected Miyazaki chief {{!}} The Asahi Shimbun: Breaking News, Japan News and Analysis |url=https://www.asahi.com/ajw/articles/14808701 |access-date=2023-10-25 |website=The Asahi Shimbun |language=en}} It has its own [[Chinju no Mori]] with feral roosters living in it{{Cite web |title=The Miyazaki Shrine Forest - Part 3 - Miyazaki |url=https://en.japantravel.com/miyazaki/the-miyazaki-shrine-forest-part-3/5468 |access-date=2023-04-23 |website=JapanTravel |date=8 July 2013 |language=en}} Sand was dedicated to it{{Cite news |date=2023-08-15 |title=Iwo Jima sand returned 'home' to relatives of WWII Japanese soldiers via US researcher |language=en |work=Mainichi Daily News |url=https://mainichi.jp/english/articles/20230815/p2a/00m/0na/011000c |access-date=2023-10-25}} {{ill|Miyazaki Gokoku Shrine|ja|宮崎縣護國神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Miyazaki Gokoku Shrine|qq=}} , alongside {{ill|Kumamoto Gokoku Shrine|ja|熊本県護国神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumamoto Gokoku Shrine|qq=}} were completed after World War II, when the war ended and after the [[:en:Ministry of home affairs|Ministry of Home Affairs]] was abolished, so they were not designated as {{ill|Gokoku shrines|en|Gokoku shrines|qid=Q1534477|lt=Gokoku shrines|1=Gokoku Shrines}} by the Minister of Home Affairs and are actually correctly designated as "equivalent to designated Gokoku-jinja Shrine". == See Also == * [[Controversies surrounding Yasukuni Shrine]] * {{ill|Martyrs' shrines (China)|en|Martyrs' shrines (China)|qid=Q6800982|lt=Martyr's Shrines|1=Martyrs' shrines (China)}} * {{ill|Martial temple|en|Martial temple|qid=Q11124210|lt=Martial temple|1=Martial temple}} and {{ill|Wen Wu temple|en|Wen Wu temple|qid=Q7231588|lt=Wen Wu temple|1=Wen Wu temple}} * {{ill|National Revolutionary Martyrs' Shrine|en|National Revolutionary Martyrs' Shrine|qid=Q5956488|lt=National Revolutionary Martyrs' Shrine|1=National Revolutionary Martyrs' Shrine}} * {{ill|Eternal Spring Shrine|en|Eternal Spring Shrine|qid=Q4501089|lt=Eternal Spring Shrine|1=Eternal Spring Shrine}} * {{ill|Chinese Cultural Renaissance|en|Chinese Cultural Renaissance|qid=Q1073969|lt=Chinese Cultural Renaissance|1=Chinese Cultural Renaissance}} * [[Ancestral shrine]] * {{ill|Gallant Garden|en|Gallant Garden|qid=Q5518867|lt=Gallant Garden|1=Gallant Garden}} * {{ill|Gokoku shrines|en|Gokoku shrines|qid=Q1534477|lt=Gokoku shrines|1=Gokoku Shrines}} * {{ill|Tomb of the Unknown Soldier|en|Tomb of the Unknown Soldier|qid=Q1541043|lt=Tomb of the Unknown Soldier|1=Tomb of the Unknown Soldier}} * {{ill|Arlington National Cemetery|en|Arlington National Cemetery|qid=Q216344|lt=Arlington National Cemetery|1=Arlington National Cemetery}} * {{ill|Valhalla|en|Valhalla|qid=Q177860|lt=Valhalla|1=Valhalla}} (home to the souls of fallen warriors in Scandinavian mythology) * {{ill|Walhalla (memorial)|en|Walhalla (memorial)|qid=Q321240|lt=Walhalla Shrine|1=Walhalla (memorial)}} (a hall of fame in Germany honoring "commendable and honorable Germans") * {{ill|Eternal Spring Shrine|en|Eternal Spring Shrine|qid=Q4501089|lt=Eternal Spring Shrine|1=Eternal Spring Shrine}} * {{ill|The common end of myriad good deeds|en|The common end of myriad good deeds|qid=Q9609095|lt=The common end of myriad good deeds|1=The common end of myriad good deeds}} * {{ill|Greek hero cult|en|Greek hero cult|qid=Q1878906|lt=Greek hero cult|1=Greek hero cult}} {{Citation needed|date=2017-08-28}} {{Infobox religious building|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Miyazaki Gokoku Shrine | image = [[File:Haiden of Miyazaki Prefecture Gokoku Shrine.jpg|250px|Miyazaki Gokoku Shrine]]
Haiden (March 2017) | map_type = Japan Miyazaki | latd = 31 | latm = 56 | lats = 13.67 | latNS = N | longd = 131 | longm = 25 | longs = 18.35 | longEW = E | coordinates_display = inline,title | coordinates_region = JP | location = 2-4-3 Jingu, Miyazaki City, Miyazaki Prefecture | deity = Spirits of those who served the nation and public from Miyazaki Prefecture, totaling 41,874 (as of March 2016)"70th Anniversary of the End of the Greater East Asia War" Memorial Book, p. 8 | founded = March 10, 1955 | annual_festival = April 10 | website = [https://miyazaki-gokoku.jp/ Miyazaki Gokoku Shrine] }} [[File:Miyazakiken-Gokoku-jinja, Mitama festival 01.jpg|thumb|250px|Lanterns for the Mitama festival in the shrine grounds]] {{nihongo|'''Miyazaki Gokoku Shrine'''|宮崎縣護國神社|Miyazaki-ken Gokoku Jinja}} is a {{ill|Gokoku Shrines|lt=Gokoku Shrines|lt=Gokoku shrine|en|護国神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gokoku shrine|qq=}} located in {{ill|Miyazaki, Miyazaki|en|宮崎市|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Miyazaki, Miyazaki|qq=}}. The shrine enshrines the spirits of 41,820 individuals who served the nation and public from Miyazaki Prefecture since the {{nihongo|Boshin War|戊辰戦争|Boshin sensō}}, along with 54 public servants who died in the line of duty (as of March 2016). == Overview == Most of the Gokoku shrines across Japan were originally established as {{ill|Gokoku Shrines|lt=Gokoku Shrines|lt=Shokonsha|en|招魂社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shokonsha|qq=}} during the {{nihongo|Meiji Restoration|明治維新|Meiji Ishin}}, with rituals for memorial services conducted before World War II. However, due to the fragmented state of Miyazaki Prefecture during the Meiji RestorationAt the time, the Miyazaki area ({{nihongo|Hyūga Province|日向国|Hyūga no kuni}}) was divided into various territories such as the {{nihongo|Bakufu|天領|}}, {{nihongo|Nobeoka Domain|延岡藩|}}, {{nihongo|Takanabe Domain|高鍋藩|}}, {{nihongo|Sadowara Domain|佐土原藩|}}, {{nihongo|Obi Domain|飫肥藩|}}, and {{nihongo|Satsuma Domain|薩摩藩|}}., there was no single Shokonsha to enshrine the spirits of those who died for the nation across the entire prefecture. Instead, small portable shrines were used for annual memorial services. Before World War II, there were seven Shokonsha shrines in Miyazaki Prefecture"Sociologica 27", pp. 36-37. They were all renamed Gokoku shrines on April 1, 1939, following an Interior Ministry order"Renaming Shokonsha to Gokoku Shrines" (Showa 14 Interior Ministry Ordinance No. 12) "Official Gazette" No. 3656, March 15, 1939, p. 510 ({{NDLJP|2960149/2}}). However, none of them were designated by the Interior Minister as official Gokoku shrines and were instead considered unofficial Gokoku shrines"Number of Gokoku Shrines" "Outline of Interior Ministry Affairs Showa 16", Interior Ministry, February 20, 1942, pp. 375-376 ({{NDLJP|1450040/571}}). {| class="wikitable" ! Shrine name (as of March 1942) ! location (then) ! location (now) ! Notes |- | Hirose Gokoku Shrine | Kitanaka District, Hirose Village | Sadowara Town, Miyazaki City | Originally Naka Shimoshima Shokonsha, founded by Sadowara Domain Lord {{nihongo|Shimazu Tadaoki|島津忠寛|}} |- | Fukushima Gokoku Shrine | Minaminaka District, Fukushima Town | Nishikata, Kushima City | Originally Nishikata Shokonsha, located on the rear mound of Fukushima Kofun No. 4 (Kenjōzuka) |- | Miyakonojo Gokoku Shrine | Miyamaru Town, Miyakonojo City | Komatsubara Town, Miyakonojo City (abolished) | Originally Miyamaru Shokonsha, dissolved as a religious corporation on October 2, 1956"Eighty Years of Miyazaki Prefecture Governance, Vol. 1", Miyazaki Prefecture, March 31, 1967, p. 883 ({{NDLJP|3030426/510}}) |- | Takaoka Gokoku Shrine | Higashimorokata District, Takaoka Town | Takaoka Town, Miyazaki City | Originally Morokata Uchiyama Shokonsha |- | Oyamada Gokoku Shrine (Muka Gokoku Shrine) | Higashimorokata District, Muka Village | Takaoka Town, Miyazaki City | Originally Morokata Muka Shokonsha, founded at Muka Castle site |- | Takanabe Gokoku Shrine | Koyu District, Takanabe Town | Same | Originally Takanabe Shokonsha, founded at Takanabe Castle site by Takanabe Domain |- | Aya Gokoku Shrine | Higashimorokata District, Aya VillageAya Town was established as a town on October 1, 1932, but the source follows the original designation. | Aya Town, Higashimorokata District | Privately established Shokonsha |} With the outbreak of the {{nihongo|Greater East Asia War|大東亜戦争|Daitōa sensō}} on December 8, 1941, the number of war dead increased significantly, deepening the public’s respect and gratitude for the spirits of the fallen. Consequently, the governor of Miyazaki Prefecture, {{nihongo|Nagafune Katsumi|長船克己|}}, and 58 representatives from various sectors formed the Miyazaki Prefecture Gokoku Shrine Construction Association. Although there were calls to establish the shrine in different parts of the prefecture, it was ultimately decided to build it in the prefectural capital, Miyazaki City, which lacked a designated Gokoku Shrine. On April 23, 1943, Interior Minister {{nihongo|Ando Kisaburo|安藤紀三郎|}} approved the establishment of the Miyazaki Gokoku Shrine to enshrine 3,358 spirits, with the groundbreaking ceremony held on May 10, 1943. Construction continued despite laborious efforts and material shortages, but the project was halted on May 31, 1945, due to intensified air raids. Following Japan’s defeat, the shrine construction was forcibly abandoned under the orders of the GHQ’s Miyazaki Civil Affairs Chief, Navy Lieutenant Masman. After the Treaty of San Francisco in 1952, efforts to rebuild the shrine resumed. Governor {{nihongo|Tanaka Nagashige|田中長茂|}} led the reorganization of the Miyazaki Prefecture Gokoku Shrine Rebuilding Association, which secured public donations to fund the project. On June 13, 1954, it was decided to establish the shrine within the precincts of {{nihongo|Miyazaki Jingu|宮崎神宮|}}, and the shrine was officially created on March 10, 1955. Subsequently, it was registered as a religious corporation on December 24, 1955[https://www.m-shinsei.jp/m_shrine/宮崎県護國神社（みやざきけんごこくじんじゃ）/ Miyazaki Gokoku Shrine (宮崎県護國神社)] Miyazaki Shrine Introduction Site, Miyazaki Prefecture Shinto Youth Association, April 20, 2012. == Facilities == [[File:View in Miyazaki Prefecture Gokoku Shrine.jpg|thumb|250px|Memorial monument within Miyazaki Gokoku Shrine (March 2017)]] * Relics Hall * Memorial monument for the war dead from the {{ill|Bougainville Island|en|ブーゲンビル島|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Bougainville Island|qq=}} campaign * Monument for war dead from the 1st Taiwan Infantry Regiment from Miyazaki Prefecture * Monument for the war dead from the {{ill|37th Division (Imperial Japanese Army)|en|第37師団 (日本軍)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/37th Division (Imperial Japanese Army)|qq=}} * Monument for the 3710th Unit * Memorial for the Five Kamikaze Heroes of Shikishima Unit, including {{nihongo|Yukio Seki|関行男|}}, {{nihongo|Iwao Nakano|中野磐雄|}}, {{nihongo|Nobuo Tani|谷暢夫|}}, {{nihongo|Hajime Nagamine|永峰肇|}}, and {{nihongo|Shigeo Oguro|大黒繁男|}} * Monument for the {{nihongo|72nd Infantry Regiment|歩兵第72連隊|}} * Statue of the Kamikaze Heroes File:Miyazakiken-Gokoku-jinja, Memorial Center 01.jpg|Relics Hall == Relation with Miyazaki Jingu == Initially built in the western forest of Miyazaki Jingu, the shrine was operated jointly with Miyazaki Jingu. However, due to the wishes of many citizens, including bereaved families, for independent management, dedicated staff were appointed in April 1972. == festivals == * Grand festival (April 10) ** Initially held on March 10 to commemorate the shrine’s founding and enshrinement, but moved to April 10 in 1978 to accommodate better attendance and the schedules of key officials[https://miyazakijingu.or.jp/publics/index/32/detail=1/b_id=128/r_id=340/ Memorial] Miyazaki Jingu Blog, April 11, 2018. * Mitama festival (August 14-15) * Daily Memorial Service (11:00 AM) == Nearby facilities == * {{ill|Miyazaki Jingū|en|Miyazaki Jingū|qid=Q704686|lt=Miyazaki Jingū|1=Miyazaki Jingū}} == Access == * 10-minute walk from {{nihongo|Miyazaki Jingu Station|宮崎神宮駅|}} on the {{nihongo|Nippō Main Line|日豊本線|Nippō Honsen}} * 5-minute walk from the Miyazaki Jingu bus stop on the {{nihongo|Miyazaki Kōtsū|宮崎交通|}} bus route == References == {{reflist}} == Further Reading == * {{Cite book|和書|title=大東亜戦争終結七十年記念誌 |publisher=宮崎県神道青年会 |url=https://www.m-shinsei.jp/kanri/wp-content/uploads/2022/11/70nen.pdf |date=2016-08-15}} * {{Cite journal |和書|author=森幸雄 |title=護国神社を事例とする、都市のシンボル的施設に対するイメージの変遷 |url=https://hdl.handle.net/10911/2440 |journal=Sociologica |volume=27 |issue=第1,2合併号 |publisher=[[Soka University]] |date=2003rd year 3月 |pages=25-45 |naid=110006608846}} == See Also == * {{nihongo|Kawanami Gokoku Shrine|川南護国神社|1=Kawaminami Gokoku Shrine|}} - A Gokoku shrine established in Kawanami Town, Koyu District, Miyazaki Prefecture after World War II == External links == {{Commonscat|Miyazakiken-Gokoku-jinja}} * [https://miyazaki-gokoku.jp/ Miyazaki Gokoku Shrine] - Official website * {{Facebook|miyazakigokoku|宮﨑県護国神社}} {{Gokoku Shrines}} \ {{⚠️🚨COPIED from EverybodyWiki ❗❕⚠️😡😤Please respect Licence CC-BY-SA ❗}} {{translated page|ja|宮崎縣護國神社|version=102022255|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{everybodywiki link|Miyazaki Gokoku Shrine}} [[ja:宮崎縣護國神社]] {{Commons category|Miyazakiken-Gokoku-jinja}} {{wikidata link|Q120727713}} [[Category:Gokoku shrines]] [[Category:Miyazaki, Miyazaki]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Miyazaki Prefecture]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]]

Haiden (March 2017) | map_type = Japan Miyazaki | latd = 31 | latm = 56 | lats = 13.67 | latNS = N | longd = 131 | longm = 25 | longs = 18.35 | longEW = E | coordinates_display = inline,title | coordinates_region = JP | location = 2-4-3 Jingu, Miyazaki City, Miyazaki Prefecture | deity = Spirits of those who served the nation and public from Miyazaki Prefecture, totaling 41,874 (as of March 2016)"70th Anniversary of the End of the Greater East Asia War" Memorial Book, p. 8 | founded = March 10, 1955 | annual_festival = April 10 | website = [https://miyazaki-gokoku.jp/ Miyazaki Gokoku Shrine] }} [[File:Miyazakiken-Gokoku-jinja, Mitama festival 01.jpg|thumb|250px|Lanterns for the Mitama festival in the shrine grounds]] {{nihongo|'''Miyazaki Gokoku Shrine'''|宮崎縣護國神社|Miyazaki-ken Gokoku Jinja}} is a {{ill|Gokoku Shrines|lt=Gokoku Shrines|lt=Gokoku shrine|en|護国神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gokoku shrine|qq=}} located in {{ill|Miyazaki, Miyazaki|en|宮崎市|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Miyazaki, Miyazaki|qq=}}. The shrine enshrines the spirits of 41,820 individuals who served the nation and public from Miyazaki Prefecture since the {{nihongo|Boshin War|戊辰戦争|Boshin sensō}}, along with 54 public servants who died in the line of duty (as of March 2016). == Overview == Most of the Gokoku shrines across Japan were originally established as {{ill|Gokoku Shrines|lt=Gokoku Shrines|lt=Shokonsha|en|招魂社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shokonsha|qq=}} during the {{nihongo|Meiji Restoration|明治維新|Meiji Ishin}}, with rituals for memorial services conducted before World War II. However, due to the fragmented state of Miyazaki Prefecture during the Meiji RestorationAt the time, the Miyazaki area ({{nihongo|Hyūga Province|日向国|Hyūga no kuni}}) was divided into various territories such as the {{nihongo|Bakufu|天領|}}, {{nihongo|Nobeoka Domain|延岡藩|}}, {{nihongo|Takanabe Domain|高鍋藩|}}, {{nihongo|Sadowara Domain|佐土原藩|}}, {{nihongo|Obi Domain|飫肥藩|}}, and {{nihongo|Satsuma Domain|薩摩藩|}}., there was no single Shokonsha to enshrine the spirits of those who died for the nation across the entire prefecture. Instead, small portable shrines were used for annual memorial services. Before World War II, there were seven Shokonsha shrines in Miyazaki Prefecture"Sociologica 27", pp. 36-37. They were all renamed Gokoku shrines on April 1, 1939, following an Interior Ministry order"Renaming Shokonsha to Gokoku Shrines" (Showa 14 Interior Ministry Ordinance No. 12) "Official Gazette" No. 3656, March 15, 1939, p. 510 ({{NDLJP|2960149/2}}). However, none of them were designated by the Interior Minister as official Gokoku shrines and were instead considered unofficial Gokoku shrines"Number of Gokoku Shrines" "Outline of Interior Ministry Affairs Showa 16", Interior Ministry, February 20, 1942, pp. 375-376 ({{NDLJP|1450040/571}}). {| class="wikitable" ! Shrine name (as of March 1942) ! location (then) ! location (now) ! Notes |- | Hirose Gokoku Shrine | Kitanaka District, Hirose Village | Sadowara Town, Miyazaki City | Originally Naka Shimoshima Shokonsha, founded by Sadowara Domain Lord {{nihongo|Shimazu Tadaoki|島津忠寛|}} |- | Fukushima Gokoku Shrine | Minaminaka District, Fukushima Town | Nishikata, Kushima City | Originally Nishikata Shokonsha, located on the rear mound of Fukushima Kofun No. 4 (Kenjōzuka) |- | Miyakonojo Gokoku Shrine | Miyamaru Town, Miyakonojo City | Komatsubara Town, Miyakonojo City (abolished) | Originally Miyamaru Shokonsha, dissolved as a religious corporation on October 2, 1956"Eighty Years of Miyazaki Prefecture Governance, Vol. 1", Miyazaki Prefecture, March 31, 1967, p. 883 ({{NDLJP|3030426/510}}) |- | Takaoka Gokoku Shrine | Higashimorokata District, Takaoka Town | Takaoka Town, Miyazaki City | Originally Morokata Uchiyama Shokonsha |- | Oyamada Gokoku Shrine (Muka Gokoku Shrine) | Higashimorokata District, Muka Village | Takaoka Town, Miyazaki City | Originally Morokata Muka Shokonsha, founded at Muka Castle site |- | Takanabe Gokoku Shrine | Koyu District, Takanabe Town | Same | Originally Takanabe Shokonsha, founded at Takanabe Castle site by Takanabe Domain |- | Aya Gokoku Shrine | Higashimorokata District, Aya VillageAya Town was established as a town on October 1, 1932, but the source follows the original designation. | Aya Town, Higashimorokata District | Privately established Shokonsha |} With the outbreak of the {{nihongo|Greater East Asia War|大東亜戦争|Daitōa sensō}} on December 8, 1941, the number of war dead increased significantly, deepening the public’s respect and gratitude for the spirits of the fallen. Consequently, the governor of Miyazaki Prefecture, {{nihongo|Nagafune Katsumi|長船克己|}}, and 58 representatives from various sectors formed the Miyazaki Prefecture Gokoku Shrine Construction Association. Although there were calls to establish the shrine in different parts of the prefecture, it was ultimately decided to build it in the prefectural capital, Miyazaki City, which lacked a designated Gokoku Shrine. On April 23, 1943, Interior Minister {{nihongo|Ando Kisaburo|安藤紀三郎|}} approved the establishment of the Miyazaki Gokoku Shrine to enshrine 3,358 spirits, with the groundbreaking ceremony held on May 10, 1943. Construction continued despite laborious efforts and material shortages, but the project was halted on May 31, 1945, due to intensified air raids. Following Japan’s defeat, the shrine construction was forcibly abandoned under the orders of the GHQ’s Miyazaki Civil Affairs Chief, Navy Lieutenant Masman. After the Treaty of San Francisco in 1952, efforts to rebuild the shrine resumed. Governor {{nihongo|Tanaka Nagashige|田中長茂|}} led the reorganization of the Miyazaki Prefecture Gokoku Shrine Rebuilding Association, which secured public donations to fund the project. On June 13, 1954, it was decided to establish the shrine within the precincts of {{nihongo|Miyazaki Jingu|宮崎神宮|}}, and the shrine was officially created on March 10, 1955. Subsequently, it was registered as a religious corporation on December 24, 1955[https://www.m-shinsei.jp/m_shrine/宮崎県護國神社（みやざきけんごこくじんじゃ）/ Miyazaki Gokoku Shrine (宮崎県護國神社)] Miyazaki Shrine Introduction Site, Miyazaki Prefecture Shinto Youth Association, April 20, 2012. == Facilities == [[File:View in Miyazaki Prefecture Gokoku Shrine.jpg|thumb|250px|Memorial monument within Miyazaki Gokoku Shrine (March 2017)]] * Relics Hall * Memorial monument for the war dead from the {{ill|Bougainville Island|en|ブーゲンビル島|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Bougainville Island|qq=}} campaign * Monument for war dead from the 1st Taiwan Infantry Regiment from Miyazaki Prefecture * Monument for the war dead from the {{ill|37th Division (Imperial Japanese Army)|en|第37師団 (日本軍)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/37th Division (Imperial Japanese Army)|qq=}} * Monument for the 3710th Unit * Memorial for the Five Kamikaze Heroes of Shikishima Unit, including {{nihongo|Yukio Seki|関行男|}}, {{nihongo|Iwao Nakano|中野磐雄|}}, {{nihongo|Nobuo Tani|谷暢夫|}}, {{nihongo|Hajime Nagamine|永峰肇|}}, and {{nihongo|Shigeo Oguro|大黒繁男|}} * Monument for the {{nihongo|72nd Infantry Regiment|歩兵第72連隊|}} * Statue of the Kamikaze Heroes File:Miyazakiken-Gokoku-jinja, Memorial Center 01.jpg|Relics Hall == Relation with Miyazaki Jingu == Initially built in the western forest of Miyazaki Jingu, the shrine was operated jointly with Miyazaki Jingu. However, due to the wishes of many citizens, including bereaved families, for independent management, dedicated staff were appointed in April 1972. == festivals == * Grand festival (April 10) ** Initially held on March 10 to commemorate the shrine’s founding and enshrinement, but moved to April 10 in 1978 to accommodate better attendance and the schedules of key officials[https://miyazakijingu.or.jp/publics/index/32/detail=1/b_id=128/r_id=340/ Memorial] Miyazaki Jingu Blog, April 11, 2018. * Mitama festival (August 14-15) * Daily Memorial Service (11:00 AM) == Nearby facilities == * {{ill|Miyazaki Jingū|en|Miyazaki Jingū|qid=Q704686|lt=Miyazaki Jingū|1=Miyazaki Jingū}} == Access == * 10-minute walk from {{nihongo|Miyazaki Jingu Station|宮崎神宮駅|}} on the {{nihongo|Nippō Main Line|日豊本線|Nippō Honsen}} * 5-minute walk from the Miyazaki Jingu bus stop on the {{nihongo|Miyazaki Kōtsū|宮崎交通|}} bus route == References == {{reflist}} == Further Reading == * {{Cite book|和書|title=大東亜戦争終結七十年記念誌 |publisher=宮崎県神道青年会 |url=https://www.m-shinsei.jp/kanri/wp-content/uploads/2022/11/70nen.pdf |date=2016-08-15}} * {{Cite journal |和書|author=森幸雄 |title=護国神社を事例とする、都市のシンボル的施設に対するイメージの変遷 |url=https://hdl.handle.net/10911/2440 |journal=Sociologica |volume=27 |issue=第1,2合併号 |publisher=[[Soka University]] |date=2003rd year 3月 |pages=25-45 |naid=110006608846}} == See Also == * {{nihongo|Kawanami Gokoku Shrine|川南護国神社|1=Kawaminami Gokoku Shrine|}} -
